channel.title: Teacher Plus
channel.description: The magazine for the contemporary teacher

item.title: Museums as gateways to experience
item.dc:creator: Usha Raman
item.content:encoded:
As teachers, we are constantly on a mission to create opportunities to stoke and nurture a sense of curiosity and wonder in our students, to get them to think beyond the mundane, to make connections across time, location, and domains, and open their minds to a variety of perspectives. Each of us is, to a large extent, trapped inside the bubbles of our own experience, our thinking structured by what we have been exposed to. Books offer one way of encountering new worlds, of inhabiting other lives, or looking under the surface of events and phenomena. But books demand an exercise of the imagination, a willingness to disconnect from the present, the material reality we inhabit, and lose ourselves in the story. It’s a shift that not everyone can make easily.

Which is how museums enter the picture. Big or small, thematic or general, museums are deliberately structured to expose one to material artefacts, or sensory experiences, that force us to make that shift. Some museums house objects of various kinds, while others construct aural, visual, tactile, or otherwise immersive experiences.

For me, museums have been like portals, as Divya Choudhary describes them. They offer an entry into a space of discovery, along the way unsettling long-held beliefs. I’ve spent hours wandering the halls of museums of natural history, awed by reconstructed skeletons and massive dinosaur bones, or hunched over glass-topped tables holding exquisitely fashioned glass flowers. I’ve also come away sobered by the violence of war, or transported by the texture of the brushstrokes on a canvas that the familiar textbook image bears no comparison to. Museums force a confrontation – with history, with nature, with technology, with ideas. They are places of memory and memorialization, but they also can become catalysts for re-thinking, as Sita Reddy notes, in her piece about the Flora Indica exhibit at the famous Kew Gardens in the UK.

When we considered doing a special issue on museums, we suspected it would be a challenge to find people who could tease out the many ways in which the topic could be explored, and related to school education, but as always, the breadth and generosity of the contributors more than addressed our apprehensions. The articles in this issue talk about museums through multiple lenses – from the process and politics of curation to how collections can be interpreted. They also talk about spaces that serve the same function as museums – the ambitious Mapusa Mogi mural, for instance, or even the traces of history we may glimpse in our old cities. The articles take us into the often invisible dynamics that go into the making of museums – the process of archiving, the assignment of ownership, the politics of labelling.

And as with every topic we begin to explore, we find the possibilities are endless, for the pleasure of learning, both for the classroom and for life. And the icing on the cake? You get to hold a rare printed issue of the magazine with all the beautiful illustrations and photographs that such a theme brings with it!
item.excerpt:encoded: As teachers, we are constantly on a mission to create opportunities to stoke and nurture a sense of curiosity and wonder in our students, to get them to think beyond the mundane, to make connections across time, location, and domains, and open their minds to a variety of perspectives.

item.title: Lessons I learned as a teacher
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
R.K. Gayathiri Devi

Teaching is not just a profession for me—it is a journey of continuous learning, self-discovery, and resilience. Over the years, my classroom has been my greatest teacher.

One of the first lessons I learned was that patience is powerful. Working with young minds, especially in early childhood education, requires immense patience. Every child learns at their own pace, and the joy of seeing even the tiniest of progresses has taught me to celebrate every effort.

I also learned that empathy builds strong bridges. As someone who faced challenges—both personal and professional—I understood early on that every student comes with their own story. When we listen, understand, and encourage, we build trust and make learning meaningful.

Creativity became my everyday tool. Limited resources never limited my ideas. I designed projects from simple things, used songs, stories, and even local culture to make learning joyful. Teaching taught me to think out-of-the-box.

Most importantly, I learned never to give up. I once cleared a government exam but lost the appointment due to corruption. It was heartbreaking. But I didn’t let that stop me. I kept studying further, set world records, wrote papers, and continued teaching with even more dedication.

Each child I teach inspires me to be better. And every obstacle I face reminds me that real teaching is about leading by example.

These are the lessons that shape me, not just as a teacher—but as a lifelong learner.

R.K. Gayathiri Devi is a dedicated early childhood educator, gold medalist in law, and a world record holder.  She believes in teaching with heart and continues to inspire young minds through innovative methods and perseverance. She can be reached a rk.gayathri07@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
R K Gayathri Devi
Teachers are expected to teach, but are there lessons that they learn? A teacher writes about the lessons she has learnt.

item.title: What true inclusion looks like
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Anwar Hussain Rayma

New Education Policy 2020 strongly emphasizes an inclusive education in all schools, so that even differently-abled children get a chance to blossom. Currently, how many schools can claim that they are inclusive in the true sense of the term?

I can proudly say that the school I work in is truly an inclusive school, where we help differently-abled children reach their potential. Special educators have a huge role to perform in such cases and in our school we have Mr. Kamran Khavra.

Kamran and I joined Adani Public School in 2010, and over these years, I have witnessed how Kamran has been instrumental in the growth of many of our special students. In this article, I narrate the story of Siddhrajsinh Jadeja, a differently-abled child with vision problems. His story is testimony to our school’s inclusive nature.

Siddhraj’s vision was compromised, but his listening skills were excellent. Siddhraj was in class three when I first noticed that he was able to tell an entire story, word to word, and recite poems soon after I sang them for the class. His classmates too began to notice this ability and they all stood up and clapped for him on one occasion when he summarized a long Gujarati story that was just read out in class. If a school wants to become truly inclusive, all its students (the normal children) too should learn to accept children with different abilities, stand by them and support them.

As Siddhraj reached higher classes and lessons became more complex, Kamran began working with him more closely introducing him to several new learning techniques and sharing materials with him. He also began sitting in Siddhraj’s classes to observe and find out how much of the lessons the boy understood. Kamran trained the rest of Siddhraj’s teachers in methods that helped Siddhraj understand lessons better. With almost the entire school working to support Siddhraj, he became so meticulous that once I remember when Siddhraj was writing a science exam and his scribe had written out the answer, I noticed that it was wrong. It seemed unlikely that Siddhraj could make a mistake like that. So out of curiosity, I read out the question again and asked if his answer was still the same. He immediately said, “Sir, my scribe must have forgotten to add this particular term here as there cannot any other answer.” And it turned out that he was right!

This kind of diligence on the part of both Siddhraj and his teachers helped him graduate his class 10 and 12 exams with grades even better than normal children in some cases-- 82 percent in the 10th and 78 percent in his 12th commerce board exams.

Siddhraj got a special award from the chairman of our institution for his achievements.

Anwar Hussain Rayma is a seasoned educator whose tenure at Adani Public School, Mundra, encompassed a remarkably diverse set of roles. Apart from primary instruction, he served as the In-Charge of Extracurricular Activities, directed plays, and taught social science to classes VI to VIII. Rayma’s practice is fundamentally grounded in child psychology and educational psychology. A dedicated practitioner of activity-based learning, his methodology consistently centred on Bloom's Taxonomy to design engaging, high-order thinking activities for students. He can be reached at anwarrayma209@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anwar Hussain Rayma
Even though inclusion in Indian schools came into focus with the National Education Policy 1986, and has been reinforced upon in every policy after, even today in 2025 not all schools are able to fulfill this requirement. However, there are a few schools who have managed to integrate children with different abilities into the mainstream to achieve true inclusion. Read on to know about one such school.

item.title: Empowering young minds:connecting economics and business education to real-world thinking
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Jyoti Punjwani

As an economics and business teacher working with senior school students, I realize that my subject is not confined to textbooks alone—it is deeply woven into our daily lives. This makes teaching it a privilege, as it equips young learners with the skills they need to understand the world and to prepare for life beyond the classroom.

Economics and business provide students with ample opportunities to think critically, connect classroom learning with real-life experiences, and develop practical solutions to the challenges that individuals, businesses, and economies face today. Whether analyzing global economic trends or discussing everyday financial choices, students learn to think beyond conventional answers and approach problems with creativity and confidence.

One of the most rewarding aspects of teaching this subject is seeing how learners begin to apply their knowledge to their own lives. For instance, when faced with financial challenges, they start to ask: What options do I have? What strategies can I use? In doing so, they are not only developing academic skills but also preparing themselves for real-world decision-making.

Making young learners think critically about real-world economics and business
The main purpose of studying economics and business is not just to understand theories, but to evaluate ideas, question assumptions, analyze real-world events, and reflect on their impact. When my senior school learners first engaged with current affairs, they often viewed them as isolated pieces of news rather than opportunities to think critically. Through structured discussions and guided practice, they gradually began to approach these issues with an analytical mindset.

Starting to interpret and form an opinion
To cultivate the skill of analysis, I regularly bring current news articles into class—for example, “Trump imposes 50% tariff on Indian exports.” With such articles, I guide students through a process of questioning and reasoning:

What is the core issue presented?

Who are the stakeholders and how might each be affected?

How will this decision impact the economy and businesses in the short and long run?

What alternative strategies could the government adopt to reduce negative impacts?

If I were a policymaker or a business owner, what steps would I take?

By debating these questions, learners are encouraged to look at problems from multiple perspectives and develop their own evidence-based opinions.

Why real-world application matters
Engaging with real economic and business issues helps learners connect theory to practice, moving beyond memorization to critical application. They learn how policies and decisions affect trade, employment, and investment. More importantly, they begin to see themselves as problem-solvers who can propose solutions, whether for national-level challenges or personal financial decisions.

This approach not only strengthens their analytical and debating skills but also prepares them for life beyond school. It equips them with the 21st century competencies of critical thinking, adaptability, and decision-making—skills they will carry into their future studies, careers, and personal lives.

Jyoti Punjwani is an economics and business teacher in Inventure Academy, Bengaluru. with a passion for guiding young learners to see how these subjects shape the world around them. She can be reached at jyotipunjwani100@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Jyoti Punjwani
Learning, unless connected to real life, is of not much use. A teacher shares how she instils critical thinking, adaptability and decision-making skills in her students while connecting and applying her subject to real life.

item.title: Museums and monuments as teaching aids
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Roshen Dalal

The first view of the Taj Mahal with its brilliant whiteness and grand proportions is breathtaking. Even if you have seen hundreds of photographs and videos of it, it doesn’t prepare you for the original. It is the same with artefacts in museums. The picture of the so-called Dancing Girl of Mohenjo-Daro adorns many textbooks, but only when you see it do you realize how small, and yet how graceful, the image is. The tiny, 10.5 cm tall, original statue of this girl is preserved in the National Museum in New Delhi. And a walk through this museum, is really a walk through 5000 years of India’s history. As you approach the museum, you come across a metallic rock, a replica of the Mauryan emperor Ashoka’s edicts at Girnar in Gujarat, edicts in the ancient Brahmi script, that tell us about his life and times. The ancient script is not difficult to read, and students could learn how to decipher it. Entering the museum, there are a number of galleries on different floors, showcasing stone tools, ancient pottery, beautiful sculptures of deities, historic weapons, paintings, textiles, coins and inscriptions, early manuscripts, and much more. It even has sections on Central Asian antiquities and pre-Columbian art. The museum has a collection of 200,000 items, but not all can be displayed at the same time. At times, they reveal some of their hidden stock, either by replacing some items, or during special displays. So, if you happen to live in Delhi, or are visiting it, a visit to the National Museum is a must, as it encapsulates and brings to life the history of the nation. There are numerous other museums in Delhi that can be visited with students, including the Humayun Museum and the National Gallery of Modern Art.

Every major city in India, and several minor ones, also have museums, which display ancient, medieval, or modern artefacts. At Mathura, images of the Kushana period, dated from the 1st to the 3rd centuries CE, were found in thousands. Many of these are displayed in Mathura Museum, reflecting Buddhism, Jainism, and Hinduism. A teacher can select some of the lesser-known images while going through the museum and explain their history and context. For instance, one will find several images of Kubera and Hariti. Students can then try to answer additional questions. Who is Kubera, and why is he associated with wealth? What type of mother goddess is Hariti? Why are they together? Are they Hindu or Buddhist? What is significant about Hariti standing to the left or the right of Kubera? Does it indicate something about the position of women? What stone is used in making the images? Where was it brought from? What kind of transport was used?

Then there is the little-known Jain deity Naigamesha. Why does he have a goat head? What could that signify? These types of questions will get the students interested in discovering more.

Whichever museum one visits, whether it is the Victoria Memorial Museum in Kolkata, the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sanghralaya in Mumbai, the Government Museum in Chennai, or any other, choosing a few artefacts and asking students to discover more about them, generates interest. The Victoria Memorial has artefacts of the British period. A virtual tour of the white marble Victoria Memorial Hall and its gardens, is also available. The Mumbai museum has historic items of the Harappan, Mauryan, and later periods, and an extensive collection of miniature paintings, among other items. Bronze images of the Chola period can be viewed in the Government Museum at Chennai, leading to learning and discussion on the Cholas and on the growth of bronze and other technologies. It has different sections on art, archaeology, and anthropology, along with a Children’s Museum.

Another category of museums are site museums, set up at excavated archaeological sites, such as Hastinapur or Purana Qila. These contain ancient pottery, coins, and inscriptions, and offer a great learning opportunity to understand how cities are buried by wind and sand, and how archaeological excavations take place.

There are some museums that specifically focus on one area of history, such as the Humayun’s Tomb Museum in New Delhi, or the site museum at Red Fort. The Vijayanagara Empire is showcased in the Archaeological Museum at Kamalapur, Hampi.

Many museums also include artefacts of the British period and of the kings, nawabs, and rulers of those times, and there are also private museums, with items collected by individuals from various parts of the world. One such is the Salar Jung Museum in Hyderabad.

Museums have also been set up around the period after independence. A significant museum of modern times is the Partition Museum at Amritsar, which collects records and memories of the Partition of India and Pakistan in 1947. Based on this, senior students can be involved in a discussion on how and why the partition took place, along with the related issues of religion, caste, and identity. Another branch of the Partition Museum is in Delhi.

Museums are not just about political history, but can be about art, various crafts, textiles, and other aspects of the past and present. There are museums specifically explaining tribal culture.

Not every city, town, or village has a museum. But even the smallest place has a monument of some kind, whether it is a temple, church, mosque, gurdwara, or dargah. The art and architecture of these can be studied, along with associated inscriptions. It leads to the study of kings or officials responsible for erecting them, and the historical context. Visits to Ajanta and Ellora caves, the Kandariya Mahadeva temple, Elephanta caves, the grand Chola temples, or to historic gurudwaras and mosques, are all tools to interest students in history. Rock cut caves of the eastern or western ghats are another category. And even the smallest shrine in a village, has a history and a wider social context that explains a lot about cultural practices and social history.

Memorial monuments are also worth studying. Some are ancient, providing records of people killed in battle, and sometimes even of dogs and horses, but there are relatively new monuments as well, erected to commemorate victories, or to list the victims. Among these, notable is the National War Memorial at India Gate, New Delhi, and the War Heroes Memorial at Amritsar. The National War Memorial was founded in 1972, but a new and extensive memorial covering 40 acres, with a central obelisk, was inaugurated in 2019, containing the Amar Jawan Jyoti, or eternal flame of the martyred soldiers. At Amritsar is the Punjab State War Heroes’ Memorial and Museum, which records the history of battles and warriors of Punjab. An iconic 45-metre-high steel sword in the central courtyard, is a unique sight. These bring the history of wars and battles alive, and could be a starting point to discuss when and whether wars are required, and also the tragedies resulting from war. These could open further discussions on the origins of nations, boundaries, and the sense of nationality, followed by a deep dive into contemporary wars.

Other types of monuments are statues of people of the past. Statues are created of modern-day political figures, but also of artists and writers. There are historic statues too. Among them, the statue of King Kanishka, now headless, dates to the 2nd century CE. An interesting historical statue, dating to the 10th century, is that of Bahubali, also known as Gommateshwara, a Jain saint. This monolithic statue, carved from granite, stands on Indragiri hill in Sravana Belgola in Karnataka, and is 17 metres high. Bahubali, after defeating his brother Bharata in battle, gave up his kingdom and became an ascetic. Based on this, there could be discussions on earthly and other types of power. Also, what does this monument tell us about Jainism and the ministers and kings who practiced it?

Though an actual visit is best and most inspiring, virtual visits, through videos and photographs, also prove interesting for students. Small videos can be made of an artefact, painting, or monument, with comments and questions from students and teachers.

There is a lot of scope for creating such videos in India, though some already exist and can be viewed on YouTube. There are also museums all over the world which can be accessed virtually and some of them also have items from India. They are additionally great for extending the knowledge of students beyond India, to the history of the world. The Metropolitan Museum in New York has great videos under the section #metkids, apart from those for adults.

Libraries with old manuscripts are also a fascinating source of history, and viewing them can be inspiring.

To summarize, visiting a museum is a great way to gain historical knowledge. The teacher can provide basic information and ask the students to follow it up, do some research, and present it to the class. If there are no museums in the area, one can visit local religious or other monuments, and also view YouTube or other videos. In case internet connectivity is not available or poor, which is the case in several villages and remote areas in India, the teacher can use large photographs pasted on cardboard and realistic replicas. It can even be a class exercise to create these. A small museum too, of items made by students or collected from different areas, can be set up. All this provides an extra dimension to the study of history.

There are hundreds of museums and millions of monuments of different kinds across India. This article just touches on a few of them and indicates how they can be used to make the teaching of history more interesting and colourful.

Roshen Dalal has a PhD in ancient Indian history from JNU, New Delhi. She is the author of 15 books on history, religion, and philosophy, including seven books on history for youngsters. She lives in Dehradun. She can be reached at roshendalal@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Roshen Dalal
Whether it’s a walk through the National Museum or a visit to a temple, cave, or memorial; heritage spaces allow us to think about India’s culture, religion, technology, and power. Roshen Dalal writes about how these spaces can become useful teaching tools in the classroom.

item.title: Beyond the glass case: turning museum trips into scientific expeditions
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Mahak Khanna

"Science is a way of thinking much more than it is a body of knowledge." – Carl Sagan

We’ve all been there. The annual class trip to a science or historical museum. The circulars have finally been returned, the buses are waiting, and you stand with your group of students at the crowded museum entrance. You take a headcount for the tenth time, trying to keep your neat double-file line from dissolving into the general excitement as your students gaze, wide-eyed, at the day ahead.A few students already have a notebook in hand, making notes of new things they observe during the trip. Others are simply asking, "Is it time for lunch yet?" The day, meant to be a pinnacle of experiential learning, can sometimes feel like a chaotic exercise in crowd control.

But what if we could transform this experience? What if, instead of being a passive field trip, the museum visit became an active scientific expedition? Imagine a different scene: a small group of students are huddled around a fossil display, not just looking, but seeing. One is sketching the intricate pattern of a trilobite’s shell in a notepad. Another is pointing, debating with a friend: “Why do you think its eyes are on top of its head?” Students aren't just consuming facts, they are behaving like scientists.

This shift is more than just a change in activity; it's a fundamental change in mindset. It’s about reframing the museum from a temple of established facts into a living laboratory of clues, a cabinet of evidence waiting to be interrogated.

The core shift: from answers to evidence

The fundamental difference between learning science from a textbook and learning it in a museum lies in the distinction between answers and evidence. A textbook might tell us, “The Stegosaurus had bony plates along its back, likely for thermoregulation or display.” This is an answer, a neat conclusion. A museum, however, shows us the evidence: the hulking, fossilized skeleton itself. Suddenly, the conclusion isn't so neat. Students can see the size, shape, and texture of the plates. They can see the network of grooves where blood vessels might have run. This tangible evidence invites questions that the textbook’s statement had already closed: How do we know they were for display? Could they have been for defence? Why are they so thin if they were armour? The museum doesn’t just give the answer; it reveals the mystery behind it and invites students into the ongoing scientific conversation. Consider the difference between a textbook chapter on the Indus Valley Civilization and standing in front of a display of Harappan seals at the National Museum in New Delhi. The book might tell us, “The Harappan script remains undeciphered.” But the evidence, the small, intricate seals with their elegant animal motifs and enigmatic symbols, sparks a firestorm of authentic questions in a child's mind: "Why are all the seals so small? Was this a language, or were they just pictures? Did every family have one?" The artefact doesn't provide an answer; it provides a mystery, something a child will be interested in solving, whether by reading more or by doing an internet search. Our job as educators is to guide them in that conversation.

Why is this shift from passive viewing to active inquiry so vital? Because in doing so, we are teaching something far more valuable than the contents of a single exhibit. We are nurturing the very habits of mind that define a scientist: relentless curiosity, sharp observation, and the courage to ask questions. For the student, this transforms the museum from a lecture hall into an intellectual treasure hunt. They become scientific detectives, piecing together clues and unlocking mysteries on their own. This is where learning becomes an adventure, and that genuine spark of discovery is what makes the knowledge truly stick.

Here’s how we can structure a museum visit around the core pillars of scientific enquiry.

1. The art of seeing: Observation as a superpower

Before a scientist can ask a question, they must first observe. Museums are unparalleled training grounds for this foundational skill. They are filled with primary sources: the actual tool, the preserved specimen, the authentic fossil. We need to teach our students to look past the descriptive label and engage directly with the object itself. For example, while visiting a geology museum, instead of just seeing 'pretty rocks', they might observe the perfect hexagonal structure of a quartz crystal or the paper-thin layers of a piece of mica. The goal is to move from passive looking to active, structured observation.

Teacher takeaway: The "silent observer" activity

Before your trip, identify one or two complex, information-rich exhibits: a diorama of an ancient ecosystem, a case displaying bird beaks of different shapes, or a display of early technological devices. In the museum, bring your students to the chosen exhibit and give them this simple instruction:

“For the next 10 minutes, your only job is to observe. You are not allowed to talk. In your field notebook, write down at least 15 things you can see. These should be pure observations, not interpretations. For example, instead of writing ‘the dinosaur is angry,’ write ‘the dinosaur’s mouth is open, and its teeth are visible.’”

This exercise forces students to slow down and look deeply. They will start to notice the kind of details that tell a story: the smooth groove worn into a grindstone by countless hands, the precise serrations on a predator’s tooth, or the delicate veins preserved in a fossilized leaf. This rich cache of raw data becomes the fuel for every subsequent step of their enquiry.

2. The question factory: Igniting the engine of science

A good observation is a seed that grows into a question. Museums, with their curated juxtapositions and narrative displays, are designed to provoke curiosity. When students see a series of hominid skulls arranged chronologically, the display silently begs the question: Why did the brain case get larger and the jaw get smaller? Or maybe why are these skulls arranged in this manner? Imagine students looking at an ancient astrolabe at the Salar Jung Museum in Hyderabad. Their observation might be of its intricate brass dials and complex markings. A simple "What?" question is, "What did this do?" But the "How?" is where the science lies: "How could sailors use the stars to know their location on a vast ocean?" This opens up a conversation about celestial mechanics, trigonometry, and the historical application of physics in navigation.

Our role is to help students articulate the questions that bubble up from their observations. Instead of a worksheet that asks them to find facts, give them one that prompts them to generate questions.

Teacher takeaway: The "question factory" worksheet

Design a simple three-part worksheet for your students to fill out as they explore. For each major gallery or key exhibit, they must generate three types of questions:

  1. A "What?" question (Clarification): This is for understanding the basics. “What is this rock made of?” or “What part of the world did this animal live in?”
  2. A "Why/How?" question (Analysis): This pushes them to think about function, cause, and effect. “Why does this bird have such a long, thin beak?” or “How did early humans make tools this sharp without metal?”
  3. A "What If?" question (Hypothesis/Creativity): This opens the door to speculative and creative thinking. “What if the dinosaurs had never gone extinct?” or “What if this tool was used for something other than what the label suggests?”

By the end of the visit, students will have something far more valuable than a list of memorized facts, a catalogue of their own curiosity.

3. From play to proof: Hypothesizing in the halls of science

Many modern science centres like the Visvesvaraya Museum in Bengaluru or the Nehru Science Centre in Mumbai are filled with interactive exhibits. It’s easy to dismiss this as mere “play,” but with the right framing, every button pushed and every lever pulled can become a micro-experiment. These exhibits are controlled environments where students can manipulate variables and observe outcomes, the very heart of the experimental process.

Teacher takeaway: The "predict-observe-explain" framework

Arm your students with this simple but powerful framework for every interactive exhibit they encounter. Before they engage with it, they must:

  1. Predict: Ask them to form a hypothesis. “I predict that if I pull this lever, the ball will travel further because…”
  2. Observe: Have them perform the action and carefully watch what happens, paying close attention to the outcome.
  3. Explain: Ask them to reconcile their prediction with the observation. “My prediction was correct/incorrect. The ball travelled further because the lever provided more mechanical advantage. I saw that a small movement on my end created a large movement on the other.”

This structure transforms aimless button-pushing into a rigorous, if miniature, scientific experiment. It teaches them that science isn’t about getting the “right” answer on the first try, but about testing ideas and refining understanding based on the results.

4. Bringing it home: Analysis, synthesis, and communication

A scientific expedition doesn't end when the team leaves the field. The most crucial work happens back at the lab, where data is analyzed, ideas are debated, and findings are shared. The learning from a museum visit must be anchored back in the classroom.

This is the time for students to sift through their observations, review their questions, and start connecting the dots. The post-visit discussion is where they transition from data collectors to sense-makers.

Teacher takeaway: The "be the curator" project

As a culminating activity, challenge students to become experts. Divide them into groups based on the galleries they found most interesting. Their task is to create a new "mini-exhibit" for the classroom. This could be a poster, a diorama in a shoebox, or a short digital presentation. The exhibit must include:

  • An "artefact": A model, a detailed drawing, or a collection of images.
  • An exhibit label: A concise paragraph explaining the artefact’s significance, based on their observations and post-visit research.
  • An engaging question: A thought-provoking question for their classmates, designed to spark the same curiosity they felt in the museum.

This project assesses a wide range of skills: synthesis of information, clear communication, and the ability to think like an educator themselves. It cements their learning by giving them ownership and purpose. To make this vision a reality, a structured approach is key.

  • Before you go: Prepare a clear mission to turn your students from passive tourists into active investigators.
  • During the visit: Facilitate discovery with probing questions rather than providing ready-made answers.
  • After the visit: Anchor the learning by having students synthesize their discoveries and connect them back to the classroom.

Museums are more than just quiet, dusty halls. They are vibrant, dynamic ecosystems of ideas and evidence. By shifting our approach, we can transform them into the ultimate training ground for the next generation of scientists, thinkers, and problem-solvers. We can teach our students that science is not a collection of facts to be memorized, but a process of curious exploration to be lived.

The next time you plan a museum visit, don't just hand out a worksheet. Hand out a field notebook and a mission. You may find you are no longer leading a class of students, but a team of scientists on the verge of their next great discovery.

Mahak Khanna is a creative storyteller and instructional designer with a passion for crafting engaging learning experiences and imaginative children’s stories. She loves to blend creativity with clarity to inspire young minds and adult learners alike. She can be reached at mahakkatyal18@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Mahak Khanna
What if a museum trip stopped being a chaotic field visit and became a scientific expedition? Mahak Khanna shares how a school museum trip can be transformed into an experience of discovery.

item.title: Understanding the past and informing the present
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aditi Misra

Stepping into a museum is like having a conversation across centuries. Museums are the guiding light to human understanding, where human experience, science, culture, and history all come together. Every artefact – be it a Harappan seal, a medieval tapestry, or an early mechanical clock – is a thread in humanity’s fabric. From the oldest cave paintings to contemporary digital installations, museums preserve memory: the beliefs, mistakes, beauty, and resilience of people. In civilizations, long before printing or formal classrooms, knowledge was carried through stories engraved on stones or embodied in craft. Temples, palaces, religious sites, and royal treasuries functioned as early museums – repositories which are reminiscent of the past.

As time passed, these collections moved from private or sacred spaces into public life. During the Age of Enlightenment, rulers in Europe opened their royal and private holdings to citizens. Collections in Asia, similarly, shifted from the royal court or religious custodianship to public display. Thus, museums evolved into institutions of learning – not merely galleries of display, but classrooms of culture. The experience of seeing and touching objects, walking among relics, hearing footsteps in ancient halls has a different kind of pedagogy than any textbook. A fossil does more than illustrate Earth’s prehistory; it helps one understand creatures that became extinct and the pernicious effects of climate change. A musical instrument engraved with weathered symbols might tell of trade routes, religious rituals, or the aspirations of generations.

Museums are dynamic environments where learning is initiated through curiosity, observation, and first-hand interaction with actual objects. This informal learning process differs from traditional classroom settings by fostering cognitive and emotional engagement. The experimental nature of museum learning, grounded in encounters with real artefacts, enhances both cognitive and affective learning, thereby enriching the overall educational experience.

The interdisciplinary approach of museums is striking. In the National Museum, New Delhi, Harappan seals, Gupta period sculptures, and Mughal miniatures coexist, offering lessons not only in art history, but in metallurgy, urban planning, trade, and religion. Similarly, the British Museum includes Egyptian mummies alongside Assyrian reliefs and African artefacts, allowing a single visit to illuminate global patterns of civilization, conquest, and cultural exchange. The visitor learns that history is not linear or isolated; it is a blend of interactions, innovations, and influences that shaped human society over millennia. Some museums actively integrate cultural performances, workshops, and storytelling sessions alongside exhibits. For instance, historical re-enactments, traditional craft demonstrations, or folk music sessions allow visitors to experience history as a living practice rather than a static display, fostering empathy and cultural sensitivity.

The educational power of museums extends into formal schooling as well. Field trips to historical museums transform passive learning into active engagement. During the Mauryan period, inscriptions on pillars and coins recorded governance, moral principles, and administrative innovations. Encountering these relics prima facie helps students understand abstract concepts like political organization, ethical governance, and economic networks, bridging the gap between textbook knowledge and lived historical reality. Such collaborative engagement mirrors real-world inquiry, where understanding emerges through dialogue, debate, and multiple perspectives. By visiting museums, learners can refine analytical skills, enhance communication, and develop a nuanced comprehension of history and culture.

Strong community partnerships are integral to the success of museum education programmes. Collaborations with schools, universities, NGOs, and local authorities enable museums to design outreach initiatives that address the needs of specific populations. The Salar Jung Museum in Hyderabad, for instance, collaborates closely with local schools to organize educational camps and art appreciation workshops, while the Nehru Science Centre in Mumbai partners with science educators to deliver curriculum-based modules and interactive demonstrations.

Digital innovation has further amplified museums’ learning potential. Virtual tours of the Louvre, Smithsonian, or Egyptian Museum enable students worldwide to access objects they may never see in person, yet engage meaningfully with them. AR (Augmented Reality) and VR (Virtual Reality) experiences recreate historical environments – Harappan streets, medieval courts, or battlefields allowing learners to immerse themselves in the daily life of past civilizations. This integration of technology with historical artefacts ensures that museums remain relevant to contemporary learners while preserving the authenticity of the past.

Museums also serve as vibrant spaces where learners develop analytical thinking and reflective skills. By observing artefacts, visitors are encouraged to question their origin, purpose, and context. Temple sculptures, manuscripts, and crafts reveal religious beliefs, cultural values, and artistic innovation. This hands-on engagement with tangible history helps learners move beyond rote memorization, constructing understanding through exploration and critical observation. A single object, such as a medieval astronomical instrument, can illustrate scientific knowledge, craftsmanship, trade routes, and the intellectual climate of its era. Similarly, architectural models or city plans from ancient civilizations reveal urban planning, resource management, and societal organization. By connecting multiple fields of study, museums provide a richer comprehension of human civilization, emphasizing that history, culture, and innovation are inseparable strands of the same narrative.

Preserving marginalized narratives is another vital role of museums. Local and regional museums, including tribal or community-focused institutions, display Indigenous knowledge, oral histories, and traditional crafts that are often absent in mainstream historical accounts. They provide insights into social structures, ecological practices, and cultural resilience. Students and visitors engaging with such collections gain appreciation for diverse human experiences and develop cultural empathy. This inclusive approach ensures that history is not a single, dominant story but a mosaic of multiple voices, ideas, and experiences that collectively shape humanity. One such example is the Tribal Museum in Bhopal, which showcases Indigenous knowledge systems, oral traditions, and forest-based livelihoods.

Museums also inspire creativity and imagination by encouraging learners to envision the lives of those who created the artefacts. Observing ancient tools, textiles, or sculptures prompts questions about everyday life, rituals, and social interactions in historical contexts. For instance, examining a Gupta-era sculpture may lead one to consider not only its aesthetic qualities but also the religious, political, and technological factors that influenced its creation. Similarly, studying Harappan seals or pottery designs encourages reflection on trade, symbolism, and communication in early urban societies. Through such inquiry, learners develop critical thinking, interpretive skills, and an appreciation for the complexities of human history.

In addition, museums cultivate civic and social awareness. Exhibits about freedom movements, cultural heritage, and social reform illustrate how communities responded to challenges, embraced innovation, and pursued justice. Learning from such examples encourages reflection on contemporary social issues and personal responsibility. Museums, therefore, function as forums where the past informs present action, helping learners understand the consequences of human choices and the value of ethical leadership.

In conclusion, museums are far more than repositories of objects. They are active learning spaces that pave the way to know the unknown. They transform observation into inquiry, emotion into understanding, and individual curiosity into collective insight. From Harappan seals to Mughal miniatures, from tribal crafts to medieval manuscripts, each artefact carries stories, lessons, and reflections that surpass time. By preserving memory, celebrating diversity, fostering critical thinking, and connecting multiple disciplines, museums ensure that history remains alive, relevant, and instructive. They invite learners not only to understand the past but also to engage with it thoughtfully, drawing inspiration and wisdom that informs present and future actions. In every hall, gallery, and exhibition, museums continue to bridge centuries, offering a space where human achievement, resilience, and creativity endure as guiding lights for generations to come.

Aditi Misra is Director of three schools following the National and IB curriculum, an award-winning educator, author, CII Education Panel member, British Council Ambassador, and CBSE honouree. She can be reached at principal@dpsgurgaon.org.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aditi Misra
Museums present the coming together of human belief, craft, science, and memory. Aditi Misra writes about how encountering such objects firsthand nurtures important skills and values in children.

item.title: Museums as living learning spaces
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Riya Sharma

There is a quiet magic that unfolds the moment one steps into a museum. The world outside slips away and time seems to rearrange itself. The stillness is not empty – it hums with possibility. Every artefact seems to lean forward, waiting for someone to notice, to ask, to wonder: What stories have you carried across the years?

My own journey with museums began not with the grand ones we often see in books, but with smaller, local ones. I remember standing in front of a simple stone tool in a district museum – cracked, ordinary to the eye, yet powerful in the way it held an entire history of survival and skill. In that quiet space, I felt, for the first time, that learning is not always about facts, dates, or labels. Sometimes, it is about standing still and listening to what an object is trying to say.

This is what makes museums different. They are not static storerooms of the past. They are living spaces. A fossil does not sit in its case silently; it reminds us of Earth’s vast timelines. A palm-leaf manuscript doesn’t just preserve words; it whispers of hands that wrote it under fading oil lamps. Even a coin, dull and weathered, connects us to journeys, trades, and conversations of people who walked the same ground, centuries before us.

Whether it is the Indian Museum in Kolkata or the National Museum in Delhi, museums show us how diverse and layered our histories are – from Harappan seals to Mughal paintings, from tribal masks to contemporary art. In these halls, you don’t just see “objects.” You feel their presence. You sense belonging. Standing there, it is impossible not to realize that we are part of a larger human story, one that continues to be written every day.

My experience at the Louvre in Paris added an international dimension to this realization. Standing before the Mona Lisa, I felt the same quiet magic that smaller museums had given me – the centuries between the artist’s brush and my gaze collapsed into a single moment of connection. The grand halls, the vast collections, and even the hushed corridors seemed to invite reflection and curiosity. It reminded me that whether in Kolkata, Delhi, or Paris, museums share a universal power: to make us pause, wonder, and engage with the stories of humanity.

Museums also teach us something about the rhythm of learning. Unlike classrooms bound by timetables, a museum lets you linger where curiosity draws you. One child might stand mesmerized before a dinosaur skeleton, while another might be lost in miniature paintings. Both are learning – but at their own pace, in their own way. This gentle, unhurried rhythm is what makes museums powerful. They give us the freedom to notice details, to reflect, and to return with questions that continue to grow long after we leave.

As a teacher, this rhythm has often inspired me to bring the museum way of learning into the classroom. I ask my students to “look at” rather than “look past.” To pause with an image in their textbook, to imagine the story of a map, to trace the journey of an artefact they may have seen on a school trip. What museums remind us is that learning is not only about collecting information – it is about cultivating curiosity and making connections.

Every museum visit leaves something behind. Sometimes it is awe, sometimes a question, sometimes just a small shift in how we see the world. Together, these moments shape knowledge and perspective. They help us understand that we are not isolated learners, but participants in a shared human experience that spans centuries and cultures.

In the end, museums are not merely repositories of objects. They are living, breathing spaces of curiosity, imagination, and connection. They invite us to wander, to wonder, and to belong. And in that sense of belonging, we find that learning is not a task to be completed, but a journey to be lived – one story, one artefact, one moment of wonder at a time.

Riya Sharma is a geography teacher who strives to make learning meaningful, engaging, and connected to everyday life. She can be reached at sharmariyasharma161@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Riya Sharma
A walk through museums in Kolkata, Delhi, and Paris reveals how fossils, manuscripts, and coins connect us across time. According to Riya Sharma, museums are spaces where objects invite us to pause, wonder, and listen to the voices that continue to shape us.

item.title: The many muses of a museum
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Anand Krishnaswamy

For generations of Indian children, monuments have never been remote, glass-cased artefacts of the past. They have been lived with, climbed upon, hidden behind, and woven into the imaginative fabric of childhood. A crumbling gate becomes a boundary in a neighbourhood game of hide-and-seek. A mossy arch near home is whispered about at dusk as the site where spirits roam. A flight of worn red sandstone steps is the stage for imaginary coronations or pitched battles fought with twigs for swords. Growing up in India means that the past is not confined to textbooks – it juts abruptly into street corners, interrupts school runs, and rises in the middle of bazaars as if history cannot help but insist on being present. We do not learn history by first going to school; history ambushes us on our way there.

As a history educator, this proximity has always felt like a gift. Yet, the deeper gift arrived when I had the chance to translate these living encounters with monuments into a more deliberate pedagogic frame – under the aegis of the Education Through Monuments initiative, led by the indefatigable Sandeep Sethi, Director of Education at The City Palace, Jaipur, supported by the Maharaja Sawai Man Singh II Museum Trust. To be invited to support, speak, and design around this premise – that museums and monuments themselves can serve as classrooms – is to recognize that heritage is not merely to be conserved; it is to be repurposed intellectually and imaginatively in the service of learning.

One monument, many lenses

A museum’s artifice is unknown to the monument’s tale. The deepest misconception about monuments is that they exist merely to tell history. In reality, a single structure – a fort, palace, observatory, cenotaph, stepwell, gateway, temple, or memorial – is a polyphonic object, legible through multiple lenses:

  • Technological prowess – What materials, toolchains, and construction techniques were available and mastered?
  • Language and inscription – How do scripts and vocabulary on plaques, coins, stones, and edicts reveal thought-worlds?
  • Targets of political action – Which sites were seized, defaced, canonized, or reclaimed as political assertions?
  • Narrative and storytelling – What myths, legends, folktales, and local gossip circulate around the site?
  • Cinema and popular media – How have filmmakers, photographers, and social media creators re-cast its meaning?
  • Scientific wonders – What problems of astronomy, hydraulics, measurement, or acoustics were solved in stone?
  • Social context – Who was allowed in, kept out, celebrated, or erased by the monument?
  • Literary echoes – How are monuments referenced in poems, plays, travelogues, and diaries of their time?
  • Mathematics – What patterns of ratio, symmetry, curvature, and calculus are embedded in the form?
  • Architecture and construction – How do arch forms, domes, vaults, and courtyards discipline space?
  • Symbols and symbolism – What do motifs, colours, alignments, and emblems signify?
  • Urban/town planning – How is the monument positioned in the logic of city design, traffic, power, and memory?
  • Music and dance – Which courts, courtyards, and pavilions were acoustic and performative spaces?
  • And yes – history itself – The layered sequence of who built, used, altered, abandoned, occupied, retold, and reclassified it.

Instantly, the monument ceases to be an inert slab of the past. It becomes a prism through which the present is refracted.

Lenses become learning domains

For education, these lenses are not merely interpretive – they are curricular. Each way of reading a monument is, in fact, a gateway into a disciplinary way of thinking:

  • Evolutionary – How human needs evolve into built forms over centuries.
  • Technological – Innovation in tools, materials, forces, and processes.
  • Arts and Aesthetics – Judgement, proportion, and sensory coherence in stone.
  • Economic – Who financed, profited from, or controlled the monument?
  • Design – Intent, constraint, ergonomics, and iteration long before Computer-Aided Design (CAD) existed.
  • Urban Planning – The choreography of the city, power, water, and worship around it.
  • Social – Inclusion, exclusion, hierarchy, ceremonial access.
  • Scientific – Experimental thought-worlds encoded in structure.
  • Architectural-Mathematical – Pure thinking expressed in measurable stone.
  • Contemporary – How a monument now functions in the civic imagination.
  • Cultural and Traditional – Memory practices, rituals, and identity anchored to it.
  • Political – Every restoration, demolition, naming, or neglect is a political act.
  • Historical – The narrative arc of change over time.

Thus, the monument is not merely a source; it is a syllabus.

Literature, movies, and poetry

Whether it is Chitra Banerjee Divakaruni or erstwhile Bollywood songs, references to monuments abound. Here is a page from the book Of Power and Pity by Mulk Raj Anand. Look at how the references to monuments (major and minor) are effortlessly woven into the narrative along with Roohafza! I was surprised to read a mention of a drink that one barely treats differently from a Rasna or Limca or any of the many bottled beverages. Similarly, the references to the Taj Mahal abound in many of Bollywood’s songs (I wager it might be the most referenced monument in Indian movie songs). Indian movies have contributed significantly to popularizing monuments as well as natural wonders of India. When Mohammed Rafi, in his dulcet voice, pined for at least the shadow of the hero’s love (in the movie Mere Mehboob), that he might treat it like the Taj Mahal on a full moon night and adore it with his eyes, it brought tears to the heroine’s eyes. In more recent years, we saw Shah Rukh Khan jump through and run over trains (in the movie Ra-One) and take control of one that finally rams into the UNESCO heritage site of Victoria Terminus, renamed as the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Terminus. Entire articles, nay, books, can be written on references to monuments in Indian movies, or poetry and literature.

Indigenous technologies

The indigenous technology of the kath-khuni style – so clearly evidenced in the venerable Chehni Kothi Palace in Himachal Pradesh – represents both a sustainable and resilient building tradition. In this system, walls are erected with alternating courses of dry-stone masonry and heavy timber beams, the wooden elements interlocking at corners into a dovetailed “maanvi” joint rather than relying on nails or mortar. Locally-available deodar and slate serve as primary materials: the stone base provides mass and anchorage, while the timber layer above delivers flexibility and shock absorption. Because the structure is built to shift rather than crack under seismic forces, many kath-khuni buildings survive tremors with minimal damage. In short, the craft is not only environmentally attuned – making use of local resources and minimal energy – but time-tested in one of India’s most seismically active mountainous landscapes. Many more details can be found on the site maintained by Sahapedia.

Similar amazing facts and feats of architecture are available in marble to those who visit the Ranakpur Jain Temple near Udaipur.

Design, engineering, and urban planning

Indian monuments are a standing archive of design intelligence, architectural sophistication, and urban foresight. One does not need to look beyond Tamil Nadu to grasp this. The Brihadeeshwara Temple at Thanjavur is not merely a devotional space but an engineering thesis in granite – its soaring vimana, precise load-distribution and axial alignment demonstrate mastery without recourse to modern machinery. The Meenakshi Temple at Madurai, with its nested precincts, pillared halls, water tank, and the calibrated choreography of procession routes, is less a single structure and more a consciously designed urban organism – an entire city staged around ritual, economy, and flow. Odisha provides equal astonishment: the Sun Temple at Konark embodies cosmic mapping in stone, while the Jagannath Temple at Puri is an operating system of built form, logistics, ritual infrastructure, and civic food economies centred on the mahaprasad. Across Gujarat and Rajasthan, stepwells reveal hydraulic ingenuity and thermal logic; forts such as Golkonda and Daulatabad record military urbanism and controlled gradients of access; Mughal gardens in Delhi and Agra display a synthesis of geometry, optics, and ecology. It is true that waves of iconoclasm, colonial dismantling, and unthinking modernization have erased much – yet what survives is still a goldmine. India’s monuments are not inert relics but open-air polytechnic universities in stone, offering living lessons in structure, climate-response, circulation, ritual design, civic ordering, and symbolic thought. What remains is abundant enough to educate a civilization again – if only we return to it with trained eyes.

A monument in two time zones at once

Consider the Jantar Mantar in Delhi. The casual visitor may receive it as a historical curiosity – an 18th century astronomical observatory – a stone laboratory built to make the sky intelligible. To historians of science, it is a sophisticated design instrument where trigonometry, shadow, time, and the curvature of the Earth converse. To architects, it is sculptural geometry made civic. And to the present – to citizens, activists, and journalists – the Jantar Mantar is a stage for protest, press conferences, and dissent; a geography of the democratic imagination. It is, without contradiction, both a heritage site and a contemporary agora. Few places express so elegantly that the past does not simply precede the present; it continues inside it.

A call to teachers and other grown-ups

If we as educators, parents, and custodians of young minds limit monuments to the realm of “history chapters”, the loss is not merely cultural, but intellectual. We sever children from one of the richest interdisciplinary classrooms available to them free of cost, in open air, with unlimited entries.

What if, instead, we trained ourselves to approach every monument as a thinking object, a site where the disciplines converse? What if we deliberately rehearsed the habit of asking new questions of old stones – not for the sake of reverence, but for the sake of relevance? The work begins not by crafting clever lesson plans, but by cultivating fresh eyes.

Let us, then, recover the childlike intimacy with which we once ran behind these walls. Let us re-enter them not as tourists ticking itineraries, but as learners in the company of learners. India has never lacked monuments; it only sometimes lacks fresh gazes. May we correct that – with curiosity, rigour, and delight.

Anand Krishnaswamy is an educator with a wide range of interests including Indian Knowledge Systems. He currently serves as the Chief Education Officer at Zista 3E4I providing consulting services (all boards) to K-12 schools across India and other countries. He can be reached at anand.k@zistaeducation.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Anand Krishnaswamy
From kath-khuni structures to the Brihadeeshwara Temple and Jantar Mantar, Anand Krishnaswamy writes about how India’s monuments hold technology, design, ritual, memory, and contemporary life at once.

item.title: To pause, to observe, and to imagine
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Deepika Gupta

Any conversation on museums and education takes me back to my schooldays. On the day of a museum visit, I would wake up earlier than usual, pack my bag with lots of snacks, and look forward to a day of fun and games with my friends. I would hop on to the school bus, excited for the day. My friends and I would chat, sing, eat, and play games enroute the museum. Upon reaching the museum, however, our happiness would quickly fade away. We would all be lined up to enter the museum and asked to move in the same queue for the entire visit. If we stopped next to an exhibit to look closely, we were reprimanded and asked to move ahead quickly so that the queue could move ahead. Any curiosity or wonder I felt was brutally throttled at the outset. Consequently, neither my friends nor I would ever talk about anything we saw during the visit. We would never discuss anything with our teachers either. Visits to monuments share a similar lack of interest and our utter inability to make sense of anything.

Gradually, the school stopped organizing these visits, and instead, began taking us to amusement parks. This happened due to the belief that students enjoyed rides and water parks more than museums and monuments. This was true. We didn’t really see anything or think deeply about any object or historic site. Recounting these memories reminds me of Kumar’s (2014) observations about school children and teachers on formal excursions. He describes children on these excursions as lacking curiosity and their teachers as merely interested in maintaining immediate order and discipline. What I experienced as a child aligns well with his observation. It feels paradoxical that despite enjoying reading history as a child, I never felt excited about monuments, museums, or any old structures during school-organized visits.

Being led to think and imagine

As I grew older, my fascination for history increased but it remained centred around reading and the written word. I never thought of my present in the light of the past. I was unable to draw any connection between the past and the present. I never bothered to think about the history of where I lived and the hundreds of historic buildings that I crossed almost every day. This began to change only when I went on my first heritage walk during college.

We went to the densely populated village of Mehrauli, where beautiful old buildings co-exist with the bustling life of the city. The heritage expert led us through beautiful structures: a tomb, a baoli (stepwell), a dargah (tomb shrine of a saint), and a khanqah (a centre for spiritual gatherings). As he explained the layered history of the village and every structure that we crossed, for the first time I got a chance to slow down, observe, and think. I was led to imagine the time when these structures were not mere old buildings but a part of everyday life. I also got the opportunity to ponder upon the small details in their construction, from building materials and motifs to architectural styles and symbols. I realized that all these elements tell a lot about the past, if only one pays attention. We further went to the Zafar Mahal, a Mughal era palace that remains unknown to many and still stands (albeit hidden) in the middle of a busy city. Although the interiors of the palace stand in a ruined condition, it offers a glimpse into the lives of the later Mughal emperors and their families. The mixed architectural style is a testament to the European influence in their taste. It makes one think about the exchange of ideas during the process of colonization and the relationship between the Mughals and the East India Company officers. The intertwined and entangled character of history, life, and architecture struck me like never before. New questions began to emerge in my mind.

This experience led me to draw connections that I never knew existed. I started walking around the city for hours. I read about the places I went to, found out about the history of what I saw, and even looked for structures or other materials that did not survive the ravages of time. The process helped me pause and see the details that I never paid attention to when I was younger. I began looking at history differently. I found it in the mundane, in the everyday. I was so inspired by what I saw and felt that I soon began leading heritage walks myself. The ability to pause, observe, think, and reflect that I cultivated through these walks came to my aid when I began working as a history teacher. I wanted students to think about the past in a complex and layered manner, as having ramifications and interlinkages with the present. To help them understand the significance of the old buildings around them and to think about the way spaces have evolved through time, I began taking them for walks as well. What I learned in the process was crucial, a lesson in the pedagogy of history.

‘Near in spirit’

All educators must learn from Dewey’s (1990) formulation of history education focusing on learning from experience and active engagement with one’s environment. Dewey described history teaching as ‘an indirect sociology’ in which the educator must delve into the study of society, the processes of its becoming, and the ways in which the society is organized (p. 155). This necessitates presenting history in the classroom as dynamic, moving, and forceful. Dewey suggested that rather than gathering information in isolation from the social environment, the focus must be on how human beings lived. Learning history must involve an attempt to conceptualize the environment that these human beings lived in. According to him, this conceptualization mandates that a child first be taught ‘what is near in spirit, not with the remote’ (Ibid., p. 161).

Dewey’s ideas help understand the relevance of material remains and physical structures around the child’s neighbourhood to bring the past closer to the present. This is what I attempted to do with my students. A child’s spatial context is crucial to develop historical thinking and to stimulate imagination. Museums, monuments, or even streets could help develop those connections and lead children to ponder: What did this market look like a few hundred years ago? How did people interact with one another? Did they use money as we do today? Where did they live? How did they survive the heat in Delhi? These are a few questions that children asked me when I took them for a walk in Old Delhi. Moreover, the old beautifully carved facades amidst modern glass and steel architecture fascinated them. Even the layout of the city led them to think about town planning and its social aspects.

I began to realize how a city could work as a museum, full of exciting and wondrous things. Akin to Bettelheim’s (1980) description of the educational purpose of museums, cities can also make one marvel, wonder, and arouse curiosity. The framework of the city-as-museum brings the past closer to the child, undoing the notion of history as distant, remote, and irrelevant. Vandrewala (2025) describes cities ‘as museums, not static in nature nor curated or isolated in their being but as dynamic and pluralistic spaces where multiple cultural narratives don’t just coexist but also thrive’ (p. 3). These narratives may not be obvious at a glance, but a closer look can unravel the varied dimensions and interconnections between the past and the present.

Curating an educational experience

Everyday life can be a great resource for learning history if carefully curated into an educational experience. Taking schoolchildren for planned heritage walks stimulates their imagination. It makes them question and think deeply about everything they see, smell, and hear around them. By helping them conceptualize the physical environs of a time that is different from their own, the experience facilitates the development of historical thinking. They wonder about change/continuity with the help of the physical spaces around them.

Unfortunately, the disinterest and disconnect I experienced on field excursions as a child is still common to most school-going children and even adults. To uncover the narratives and multi-layered spaces of the city, a teacher’s role as a heritage expert and experience curator is fundamental. First, she must pause and listen to the stones before she is able to help students do the same. By being curious about what surrounds her, she will be able to develop in herself what she seeks to develop in the child. Second, she must be a storyteller. By weaving stories around buildings and spaces, the teacher can help trigger a child’s imagination and lead them to think about life in a different time. However, she must be careful to not be too instructive. She must distinguish between telling stories to help the child imagine and giving out so much information that it leads to disinterest. Finally, it is pertinent that she lets them take their time with the structures and simply observe. Looking closely and thinking about an object or structure helps one wonder and imagine, both of which are necessary to develop genuine curiosity.

Schools must also recognize their responsibility in the process. Teachers are often burdened with other administrative concerns that take up most of their time and energy. They are unable to spend any of their intellectual or creative energy towards the visit. In the process, learning takes a backseat. The school management and authorities need to understand the importance of exploring history through interaction with one’s surroundings and acknowledge the role of teachers in curating these experiences for young learners. It is imperative that these visits be organized with a serious intention to engage with historical objects, buildings, and spaces and not as a box to be ticked off in the name of field excursions. If this does not happen, the cities and stones will remain sealed as gateways to the past.

References

  • Bettelheim, B. (1980). Children, Curiosity and Museums. Roundtable Reports, 5(2), 8-13.
  • Dewey, J. (1900). The School and Society. Chicago: Chicago University Press.
  • Kumar, K. (2014). Politics of education in colonial India. Routledge.
  • Vandrewala, S. (2025). Historic Buildings as Living Collections: Cities as Museums of Cultural Narratives. Collections: A Journal for Museum and Archives Professionals, 1-5.

Deepika Gupta is a researcher at Ca’ Foscari University of Venice. She is a former history teacher and a heritage tour leader. Her areas of interest include sociology of education, pedagogy of history, and museum education. She can be reached at deepika2994@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Deepika Gupta
Drawing on memories, heritage walks, and Dewey’s ideas, Deepika Gupta makes the case for teaching history through lived environments that invite children to observe and make connections between the past and the present.

item.title: Teaching Flora Indica: Radical Pedagogy from Kew Garden’s Botanical Museum
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sita Reddy

Radical, etymological root from the Latin, radix, root

At the western edge of London, in the heart of conservative Richmond county (which museum buffs might recognize as the site of many Tudor palace museums including those of ‘mad’ King George III and Queen Charlotte), sits the 326-acre Royal Botanic Gardens Kew – one of the great botanic gardens of the Western world. Dating back to 1759, Kew Gardens is a double museum, a museum twice over: it is a living museum, the largest collection of plants and fungi, living and dried, on the planet; and it is also an archival museum holding unrivalled historical collections of herbarium specimens, botanical manuscripts, and exquisite botanical art from its former colonies. But even with the knowledge that Kew was once the epicenter of the British Empire’s global network of botanic gardens (with India as its crown jewel), it seems at first glance the farthest thing from contemporary 21st century India that one could imagine. And yet, just last month on October 11th, even the casual visitor to Kew’s Shirley Sherwood botanical art gallery would have noted that something momentous was afoot in the Indo-British museum world. Flora Indica, the first-ever exhibition of Kew’s archival 18th and 19th century Indian botanical drawings opened to much appreciation and acclaim, its subtitle – Recovering Hidden Histories of Kew’s Indian Botanical Drawings, 1790-1850 – hinting at its radical potential for museum pedagogy not just in London but around the world, and especially in India.

What can we learn from such a botanical art exhibition, a classic example of what we museologists call ‘collections-based curation’? Framing the question another way, what can such a botanical museum teach us? At one level, Kew’s botanical museum (and the resulting exhibition) offers a radical history lesson on colonialism, given that the curator Dr. Henry Noltie, a worldwide expert on Indian botanical art, was able to reassemble and reinterpret the entire collection to reveal hidden aspects – its anonymous artists and its indigenous collectors. But at another level, since botanical art is a hybrid genre that mixes art and science, specimen and artefact, its pedagogic role goes even deeper, spilling into global stories of botanical exchange, past and present. As a co-curator of Flora Indica, I offer some thoughts, using as my road map the primary functions of museums: collections, interpretation and display, and returns/restitution. Since these roughly coincide with the three narrative arcs we had conceptualized for Flora Indica, I will present these as lessons under: a) A Collection’s Life; b) Lives of Indian Artists; and c) Afterlives/Return to India.

Lesson 1: A Collection's Life: Reassembling the Archive

What makes the Kew Indian drawings so historically important to discussions around museums and their collections? What can we learn about lost, forgotten or erased histories if we trace a collection’s journey across continents? One of the most significant collections of botanical art at Kew was made in colonial India between 1790 and 1850. Over 7,500 drawings documenting the flora of South Asia were commissioned by British East India Company botanists, but created entirely by anonymous Indian artists. These works of hybrid art travelled from India to Kew by various routes – some from private collections, many transferred to the British government via the East India Company’s India Museum in Leadenhall Street. After 1858, when the British government took over the administration of India, the Museum declined and was finally abolished in 1879 when its botanical material, including the drawings, were transferred to Kew. It should be noted here that the India Museum became what we know today as the Victoria and Albert Museum, which continues to have rich collections of South Asian arts, artefacts, and manuscripts. Yet another reminder that colonial museum collections, especially in the UK (the former metropole), do not deaccession acquired objects – often they just circulate from one site to another, rarely making their way back to their country of origin.

Once these Indian botanical paintings reached Kew, they were dispersed among a worldwide series of several hundred thousand illustrations – that is, they were filed under a botanical sequence (genus, order, species), rather than geography or history. All of which made them accessible solely to botanists who used them as proxy specimens in the naming of new species, a classic case of art being used for science. This unique art-science archive was effectively ‘lost’ – or remained invisible to the rest of the world, not least to their country of origin and the descendants of the artists who created them.

Lesson 2: Lives of Indian Artists/Remembering Forgotten Names

How then to best interpret and display these collections? Handwritten notes on the drawings by commissioners, artists, and botanists reveal two centuries of colonial and botanical history. To recover these hidden histories, the Kew Indian botanical collections have been completely refigured by Dr. Henry Noltie. Over the course of six years, Dr. Noltie went through 200,000 drawings housed between the herbarium and the Kew rare book library, extracted all the pre-1850 Indian drawings, reassembled them into 15 original collections (arranged by commissioner or place of creation) using information gathered from manuscript and herbarium sources, catalogued the drawings, and gave them accession numbers for the first time. A mammoth task that could only have been done by someone like Dr. Noltie who is both a trained taxonomist and a curator with extensive experience. Cataloguing them was a slow and thorough process – the species was identified, their current names were found and updated, and links were made between the drawings and related herbarium specimens. What was most remarkable was that Dr. Noltie made attributions and named 19 artists, many of whom were formerly anonymous. At risk of repetition, this reinsertion of artist names is nothing short of pioneering, and the refigured Flora Indica archive not only begins the process of decolonization – given that they were all native Indian artists – but transforms future research, inviting artist genealogies, attributions, adding to what colonial historians call the ‘hidden hands’ literature – the fact that so many forms of colonial-era knowledge (in this case botanical) were co-created by Indian actors, yet never acknowledged.

Flora Indica charts one of many possible journeys through this reassembled archive. As co-curators, we selected 52 drawings from a total of 5000+ – one of the most challenging curatorial tasks we’ve shared in recent years, for one thing because of the sheer embarrassment of riches. These drawings are exquisite watercolors, botanically accurate, of course, but rendered with the utmost artistry, skill, and imagination. To display our selections, we were given an exhibition space in Shirley Sherwood Gallery that included four odd-shaped galleries typically reserved for archival exhibitions.
