In the first gallery – The Artists’ Room – the artists’ names were recovered, acknowledged, and celebrated as co-creators of a remarkable body of botanical knowledge. We chose to paint the walls of Gallery 1 in an off-white cream – mimicking the white-cube gallery’s requirements of minimal intrusions into spot-lit focus on the artworks. The exhibition then followed three geographical routes, introducing plants and local artists that the botanists were fortunate to encounter – each of the next three galleries painted in gradually deepening shades on the yellow-orange-red colour spectrum. Galleries 2 (turmeric) and Gallery 3 (burnt orange) were focused on the botanic gardens of Calcutta and Saharunpur as major sites for the creation of drawings. The final gallery (red) was devoted to the plants of Bengal, Bihar, and Awadh.

Lesson 3: Museum Afterlives/Who owns these artworks? Repatriating Artworks
Finally, there is the important issue of museum repatriation – a topic that must be addressed when teaching about (and with) colonial collections. Driven by changing attitudes toward colonialism, museums in the UK are finally beginning to repatriate objects to their countries of origin, with notable examples including recent returns to India and Nigeria from the Glasgow City Council, Manchester Museum, and Horniman Museum. National museums like the British Museum are more restricted due to conservative ownership laws such as the British Museum Act of 1963, which prevents deaccessioning collections unless they are damaged, duplicates, or not of public interest. ‘Duplicates’ is the legal loophole of interest here, offering the space to implement a new model of museum practice for the return of the Kew Indian drawings.

Our proposal for the India leg of the exhibition – under the provisional title Flora Indica: The Afterlives – should be seen in this context. One of our primary goals in curating Flora Indica at Kew was to “lay the groundwork for illustrations ‘going home’ to India as (digital) exhibitions to multiple Indian cities with accompanying programmes”. Given that it was drawn from colonial collections to highlight hidden histories of the Indian artists, these afterlives are not only integral sequels to Kew’s version but their moral and ethical core. Our argument relied on a museological mantra fairly common with collections-based exhibitions intended for former colonies: Even if, and especially if, the originals could not easily be returned (due to restrictive laws or conservation issues or prohibitive cost challenges), their facsimile reproductions could, and indeed should, to facilitate free and public access in the country of their birth.

Our plan to tour the Kew facsimiles relies on a tried and tested curatorial/exhibition model. In 2019, Dr. Noltie and I had collaborated to show an exhibition of botanical drawings from the Royal Botanic Garden of Edinburgh (RBGE) in India. Titled Forests and Gardens of South India, this was his exhibition, first curated at RBGE that then travelled to Chennai and Hyderabad as high-resolution facsimile prints (giclee prints, printed and framed in India). Working closely with the RBGE Library, Dr. Noltie had facilitated the free reproduction of hi-resolution prints for the Indian galleries. I helped coordinate the Hyderabad segment at two venues – Goethe Zentrum and subsequently the State Department of Archaeology – and it was a resounding success at both venues. Visitors who could read the Telugu labels were moved to tears that artists named Govindoo or Rungiah might have made these exquisite drawings not too far from where they were displayed. In our view and curatorial experience, this was the gold standard for colonial collections-based archival exhibitions with a decolonizing goal.

For the Indian facsimile tour of Kew’s Flora Indica: Afterlives we have proposed four Indian cities as venues, each of which will display the exhibition for short durations, say a month. The four cities were chosen to complement each other given their respective and unique institutional strengths in various aspects of botanical art (e.g.: education in Hyderabad, botanic garden in Kolkata, ethnographic arts in Mumbai, a science gallery in Bengaluru). For each, we propose a primary venue for exhibition display but we also hope to include city-wide accompanying programs that will enhance, animate, and amplify the message: that exquisite Indian botanical drawings created by Indian artists under colonial rule and hidden for two centuries in Kew’s botanical museum must be seen in their country of origin. If this comes to pass, it would be a small but radical step in bridging museum repatriation theory and practice, in making sure democratic access to Kew’s Indian paintings undoes some historical wrongs in colonial history, even if this is symbolic redress. As John Berger might have said, viewers will have found new ways of seeing through the art when framed in different settings. By digging deep into plant histories, the botanical drawings will have grown roots.

Dr. Sita Reddy is a writer, scholar and curator with a wide range of publications and teaching interests in botanical art and critical museum studies. Currently a Research Associate at the Smithsonian Institution, her revised book Ars Botanica: Weight of a Petal will be published by MARG magazine in 2026. She can be reached at sitared@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sita Reddy
A hidden archive of over 5,000 Indian botanical drawings is being exhibited at Kew Gardens. Sita Reddy traces the forgotten history of the makers and the exhibits, and their potential return to India.

item.title: The Mapusa Mogi Project: an exercise in collective memory
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Neha Vaddadi

Inside the parking area of the centuries old St. Jerome’s church in Mapusa, North Goa, holding up a rather steep hillside, is a massive grey retaining wall stretching a 100 metres long and 8 meters high. As I enter the space, I see that the centre of the wall is anchored by a circular mosaic and ceramic relief that catches the early light. A giant visual of two Goan women fills the 7 metre high frame: on the left – a Hindu woman holding a basket of fish, rice, and coconuts – Goa’s staples; on the right – a Catholic woman with beads of choriz (pork sausages), bottled vinegar, and poee, Goa’s beloved local bread. Atop this central medallion are glass letters in English and Devanagari that shimmer iridescently, spelling out the words Mapusa Mogi, ‘mogi’ meaning lover in Konkani. Hundreds of small holes punctuate this wall’s surface to let the monsoon water drain from the hill behind; now, sparrows dart in and out of them, pausing on ceramic fruit and fish before vanishing again. Although the parking area sits beside a busy road, the high, fortress-like walls muffle the outside noise. Inside, the space feels quieter, still. This is the site of the Mapusa Mogi Mural Project, a monumental vision for this historic market town in North Goa, conceived by comics artist and visual designer Orijit Sen. The mural is a gift from the artist to Mapusa and its people, from someone who has loved this town for over three decades.

Its purpose is both simple and vast: to document the town’s forgotten past, capture a rapidly changing present, and imagine a more hopeful, inclusive future for Mapusa.

This scale of thinking and of seeing a place whole is characteristic of Orijit Sen’s practice as an artist. Across decades, he has immersed himself in long, detailed projects that weave together ethnography, geography, and visual storytelling. From Punjab to Hyderabad to Goa, his depictions manipulate scale, proportion, form, and perspective to reframe the everyday, prompting viewers to question their habitual modes of seeing. The Mapusa Mogi Mural continues in that spirit – an ongoing (two-years and counting) experiment in drawing a city from the inside out.

The scale of the wall and its commanding position at the town’s entrance make it an irresistible canvas for a mural. As one winds up the slope into Mapusa from the highway, it’s among the first sights that meets the eye. But Mapusa is full of such walls. In the early days of the project, we walked through the town’s central streets as part of our research, noticing the numerous large buildings that stood around us, with many of their facades bare. We joked that if permission for our wall fell through, there were plenty of others waiting to be painted.

And maybe someday they will be. Across the world, entire neighbourhoods have turned into open-air galleries – from Berlin to Bogotá – where muralists and graffiti artists from all over the globe come together to transform public walls into colossal images, inviting people to walk, look, and rediscover these cities. This is happening in India too, with street art initiatives giving entire neighbourhoods a facelift. Some of those visuals are quite stunning, done by incredibly talented artists who are able to create truly imaginative visuals at such huge scales. I also think of government-led “city beautification” drives in cities like Hyderabad, painters working under the blazing sun, covering walls with neat but disconnected scenes: flowers, animals, bits of landscape, the occasional Warli motif. The effort is immense, yet the imagery often feels random, decorative, and stripped of context.

So, what then is the role of the mural, especially in the current Indian urban landscape? And what makes the Mapusa Mogi Mural different?

Ask a Mapusa local what they think of their town and this is what they might say – that all the attention goes to Panjim. Nobody really cares for Mapusa. It’s been this way for decades: dirty, polluted, crumbling. And that’s no exaggeration. Panjim is considered the jewel in Goa’s crown – full of colour and polish, with its painted and azulejo-tiled murals, outdoor installations, riverfront promenades along the Mandovi, elegant Goan-Portuguese houses, casinos glittering at night. Like much of the rest of India, Goa’s development priorities tend to concentrate resources on select urban centres that are expected to generate revenue or attract tourism, leaving smaller towns like Mapusa overlooked.

Mapusa has always been a working town, agrarian at heart, functional, full of small trades and services that keep the rest of Goa running. It might be for this reason that it is not being looked at as a place that deserves beauty, pause, or care. Yet, it’s the town that everyone depends on, for its iconic market that draws people from across Goa, even from Karnataka and Maharashtra, for its hospitals, churches, and temples, and as an important point of transit and exchange.

Despite its size, Mapusa has an astonishingly rich social and cultural fabric. The town fans out from its bustling market, climbing into hilly neighbourhoods and winding down toward the Mapusa and Moira rivers. Scattered through these lanes are exquisite old Portuguese and Goan homes, and a surprising number of Art Deco buildings constructed between the 40s and 70s, some well-maintained, others overgrown and falling apart. The entire area is surrounded by woods, streams, rivers, rice fields, and ancient khazans – a 4,000-year-old system of tidal irrigation unique to Goa.

Mapusa was once a flourishing town. Today, it struggles to keep up, weighed down by neglect, rapid urbanization, and a slow erosion of order that has bred apathy and frustration. It is easy to look at a place like Panjim and feel left behind. Yet, the manicured and exclusive model of “development” that a city like Panjim represents would, if replicated, erase the essence of a town like Mapusa. Such models often bring with them exclusivity, gentrification, followed by surveillance and control, pushing away the working and informal classes that give this town its texture and vitality.

When development comes in this form, community values, close interpersonal relationships and a sense of belonging that span generational time are the first to fade. The choriz seller, the local bread maker, the banana vendor at the corner of Axis Bank, the motorcycle pilot waiting outside Wellness Pharmacy, the umbrella repair man by the market gate – these are some of the people who sustain Mapusa’s rhythm. When one walks around Mapusa, one cannot help but notice a majority of the hustling bustling crowd greeting one another as they go about their various chores. One hears shouts and greetings from people across the road, little bits of friendly banter to lighten their load, or even longer chats as they see an opportunity to take a break from the routine of their day. These constant sounds of greetings and passing chats cut through and weave all walks of life and classes of society together. In long-standing, vibrant communities such as this, people are not merely the service they offer. In recognizing you and having the familiarity to strike a conversation when you come to avail of the service they provide, it builds what we call a community – the heartbeat of any healthy city. What Mapusa requires is not gentrified redevelopment dictated from elsewhere, but care, recognition of the interdependent nature of these relationships, and respect for what already exists. To continue doing what it does best, but supported by thoughtful and creative infrastructure that enable this level of intimate interaction.

Therefore, it is not the infrastructure itself that holds value but value lies in every citizen when they are made to feel an integral part of the community. After all, it is not just infrastructure that makes for a healthy community but the coming together of valued and appreciated citizens; even more so when development supports this way of living.

A mural, though imagined, can act as a powerful seed planted in the collective consciousness of a place. The Mapusa Mogi Mural aims to do this: to go beyond surface-level beautification and to create an image that carries meaning and lays bare the connections that make this city what it is. It is not an exercise by one artist, but a collaborative, community-oriented process. While the overall creative vision stems from one creator, its realization is woven by many hands and minds – artists, researchers, thinkers, and local residents who know and feel the pulse of the town.

At the outset, a team of 10 artists and researchers was formed. The group selected particular sections of Mapusa to focus on and spent weeks walking through its lanes, observing and documenting every aspect of its built and lived environment: buildings, terrain, people, and small human-made details that often go unnoticed. We photographed and sketched extensively, our main mode of documentation. Meanwhile, the research team began gathering stories, recognizing that understanding a town’s geography and architecture must go hand in hand with uncovering its memories. These stories, drawn from both past and present, are what give the mural its depth and soul. The team spoke to a wide range of people – old Mapusa families, traders, and vendors who have lived and worked in the town for generations. Simultaneously, design-thinking and mapping workshops were conducted in schools and colleges, inviting children to imagine Mapusa’s future and reflect on its present. Many of their insights will eventually appear in the mural.

The final artwork will portray the essence of Mapusa: the architecture with its iconic buildings that still exist and many that have disappeared, important commercial establishments and streets, old Goan/Portuguese houses, and the life within its market and most importantly all the people who make the town what it is. The mural will include thousands of people, mirroring the density and uniqueness of human presence in the town. The entire design will be printed on hundreds of ceramic tiles, a material historically rooted in Goan culture. Its visual language will interweave reality and imagination – some buildings will appear as cut-outs revealing their interiors, some parts of the town will be entirely reimagined, suggesting new possibilities: rooftops as community spaces, pedestrian-friendly streets, community libraries, clean public toilets, children’s playgrounds. There will be carnivals and parades, the making of Narkasur, Ganesh Chaturthi celebrations, church feasts and processions, seasonal shifts, and even scenes from forgotten chapters of history, such as the little-known battle between the Portuguese army and the Ranes of Goa, outside the Swiss Chapel in Mapusa. Together, these elements will form a vast, map-like panorama: a mirror in which Mapusa can glimpse itself for a moment. What happens when a people see their own reflection, reimagined with care and possibility? When they notice that the mural’s roads are calmer, that its public spaces are usable, that beauty and utility can coexist – what might that awaken? Perhaps pride, hope, or even a healthy sense of humour. Perhaps a renewed sense of belonging.

This brings me back to the larger question of the role of the mural. When created with intention, it can become a vessel for memory and history – a source of joy, imagination, and learning. It can change the way one looks at one’s own space and their place in it, stir curiosity, and serve as a form of collective learning. For the people of Mapusa, seeing themselves granted this visibility and dignity could stir something profound.

We live in a time that severely lacks guiding images. Images can be powerful in helping shape our understanding of the world and of ourselves within it. They can direct our desires, calm our fears, or inspire new ways of thinking. To spend years learning about a town intimately, translating that knowledge into drawing, and infusing it with care and empathy is an act of deep attention. The unified image that emerges from this process will hopefully continue to resonate – with locals, visitors, children, and elders alike. It may even serve as a quiet guide, reminding people that imagination, care, and belonging are still possible. This possibility is vital to the health of a city that provides a safe and nurturing space for all its citizens.

Neha Vaddadi is one of the Lead Artists on the Mapusa Mogi Project. An illustrator and graphic designer currently based in Goa, India, she collaborates with social scientists, activists, NGOs, educators, and filmmakers on a wide range of visual design projects. Trained as an architect, she enjoys drawing for herself to explore themes such as natural and urban landscapes, observational studies of people, and ancient world history among many others. She can be reached at nehavaddadi@gmail.com. You can also find her on Instagram at @nehavaddadi.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Neha Vaddadi
Long overshadowed by Panjim, the vast murals of the Mapusa Mogi Project, writes Neha Vaddadi, are all set to present a mirror to the town’s past, present, and possible futures.

item.title: From village vistas to victory: my odyssey in English mastery
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Ramesh Sandha

In the sun-scorched outskirts of South Telangana, my childhood bloomed like a fragile flower in a storm. Born to a marginalized family, I was cradled by humble parents whose unlettered hearts brimmed with courage. Telugu, my mother tongue, flowed like a gentle river, but English loomed like a towering mountain, distant and daunting.

At 11, English entered my life—a stranger with strange symbols. My teacher, stern as a winter wind, wielded words like weapons, his cane cracking at my missteps. I stumbled through reading and writing, a clumsy colt in a race I couldn’t win. Classmates’ laughter, sharp as thorns, dubbed me the “mocking   bird” of mistakes. Yet, my love for English flickered like a stubborn spark.

A beacon broke the fog when my uncle, a scholar scarred by his own lack of English, whispered, “Wrestle with English, no matter the thorns. Never surrender.” His words became my mantra.

Teacher-centric struggles: My early education was a teacher-centric tyranny. Lessons demanded rote recitation—poems stanza by stanza, essays line by line. I craved immersion, like with Telugu, but found barren drills. I felt like a seed buried too deep, yearning for sunlight but given stones. By 10th grade, I scored a heart-breaking 34 in English, one mark shy of passing. I despised the system’s shackles, but my passion burned bright.

Moments of mockery stung. Holding an English newspaper, I was jeered as “the Englishman”, my speech mocked like a jester’s jest. Yet, my uncle’s words—“Never surrender”—sang like a lighthouse in the storm. I vowed to transform trials into triumphs.

Student-centric awakening: I embraced a student-centric mindset, seeking the encouragement I lacked. Marriage brought a radiant milestone: my wife, from an English-medium background, her eloquence a polished mirror. She sculpted my pronunciation into crisp cadences and guided my grammar, turning tangled sentences into streams of clarity. I devoured radio broadcasts, movies, and stories, embracing corrections as pearls. My efforts bore fruit: a dazzling 90% in English, second topper at the mandal level. I pursued further studies—a B.Ed. and graduation from Osmania University with honors, an M.Ed. from Kakatiya University with distinction, and an MA in English from Andhra University. Today, I serve as a government school English teacher, my classroom a canvas of creativity.

Teacher-centric vs. student-centric legacy: I’ve traded teacher-centric rigidity for a student-centric sanctuary. My classroom is a garden, not a factory—students bloom through vibrant methods inspired by my struggles, my wife’s guidance, and a rich tapestry of professional growth. Completing the TESOL Core Certificate Program (TCCP) in 2023—a 140-hour blended-learning journey with a teaching practicum—I mastered foundational ESL pedagogy and adolescent learner strategies, honing methods like role-play and debates that let shy voices soar like kites. Additional TESOL certifications in Foundations, Teaching Adolescent Learners, and Integrating Technology in ESL Classrooms empowered digital diaries—blogs or podcasts—that connect English to students’ worlds. Storytelling circles, where students weave tales, mirror the immersion I craved, now enhanced with digital tools. Peer mentoring fosters growth, pairing strong speakers with novices. The TESOL Training for Trainers, a 60-hour course, equipped me to mentor peers, a country boy now touching global horizons. As a devoted reader of Teacher Plus, I’ve drawn inspiration from its pages, learning innovative strategies and stories that ignite my teaching style with creativity. Its insights fuel my methods, ensuring every learner finds inspiration, as I once yearned. Teaching is my choice, rooted in compassion, amplified by global standards.

My proudest moment sparkled like a rare dawn when NCERT called me for training, their accolades a golden crown for my perseverance. Friends gathered to cheer, their voices a warm chorus, yet my misty eyes, glistening like dew on a mournful morning, searched for my uncle—the whisperer of my dreams. His absence struck a silent note in my symphony of success—he had passed away, leaving me aching to share this triumph, a longing that lingers like an unfinished verse. Yet his mantra, “never surrender,” echoes in my heart, a guiding star that fuels my mission.

This odyssey transcends mastering English—it’s a testament to passion’s power, carving paths through stone like a relentless river. As an English teacher, armed with TESOL’s wisdom and innovative methods, I dismantle barriers, ensuring my students soar where I once stumbled. My story is a soaring song of hope, sung for every dreamer who dares to defy the odds and never surrender. To echo this melody globally, I share my journey on platforms like Medium and LinkedIn, inviting educators and dreamers worldwide to join this transformative chorus, inspired by a country boy’s leap to a global stage.

Ramesh Sandha is PGT English, TSWRS Boys, Kandukur, Rangareddy, Telangana, India. He can be reached at rameshpaulsandha@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Ramesh Sandha
It takes courage, conviction, and persistence to achieve something. Read the author's inspiring story of how he mastered a skill and is now t raining students in that same skill.

item.title: Unpacking the ‘baggage’ in museums around the world
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Luis Dias

From my childhood, I have both loved and been a little intimidated by museums.

Any connection with our human (or even intergalactic) past excites me. That is what I love about museums of all sizes and specializations. Even today, a year short of 60, I feel a little cheated if I haven’t eyeballed every last artefact or coin on display, and read the fine print accompanying each item, before I have to leave at closing time, especially if I know I might not return for a long time, or ever.

Sadly, barring very few exceptions, a visit to a generic museum in India is not an exciting prospect. More often than not, one will encounter dank, dusty cobwebbed spaces, unflatteringly illuminated exhibits with either no or at best a laconic caption, staff who themselves show no respect for the exhibits in their care, chattering loudly as if the visitor were invisible or an intrusion.

In contrast (again speaking very generally, as I’ve encountered some exceptions here too), museums are much more welcoming and user-friendly outside India.

There is one museum that I got extremely well acquainted with during the decade (1998 – 2008) I spent living and working as a doctor in England: The National Gallery, London. The fact that entry was free and its location was quite central meant that it was at various times a welcome refuge from bad weather, or just a place for quiet contemplation, or even an excuse to revisit many paintings that became my close friends due to their familiarity and power to de-stress me. The ‘friends’ are Jan van Eycks’ ‘Arnolfini portrait’, Velásquez’s ‘Rokeby Venus’, J. M. W. Turner’s ‘Rain, Steam and Speed’, and Titian’s ‘Bacchus and Ariadne’ to name just a few. Each visit helped me take notice of yet another masterpiece in the Gallery’s seemingly inexhaustible collection. I don’t remember ever leaving the building without feeling uplifted and spiritually nourished.

In general terms, however, as I grew older, questions began emerging in my mind almost inevitably from my interest in museums, about the provenance of the items on display, the manner in which the descriptions of those items in accompanying printed material or audio guides were framed, or what I felt was often not being discussed at all, or not enough.

The 2017 film Tulip Fever about an illicit love affair between a 17th century Dutch painter and a married woman whose portrait he has been commissioned to paint did not do well at the box office. But it underscored the direct link between blood money from the spice trade and how it bankrolled ‘high art’ in the so-called Dutch ‘Gold Age’, from Johannes Vermeer to Frans Hals and Rembrandt.

Two Vermeer paintings are among the eighteen from the ‘New Dutch Republic’ in Room 16 of London’s National Gallery. If one visits its website, the Room 16 collection is captioned ‘A New Art for a New Nation: Dutch Painting 1600 – 1700.’ The information below states that “The fledgling new nation prospered and witnessed the flowering of a new type of art.” It leaves the visitor to connect the dots.

I come from Goa, which is so inextricably connected to Europe’s bloody quest for spices and ‘the spice route’, so this resonates very much with me.

Earlier this year, I was in Berlin, which happens to be my birth city. I was revisiting it after almost three decades. I was keen to visit the Museumsinsel (Museum Island), a complex of five museums on Spree Island, in the city’s historical heart.

When I told my tour guide about this, she said, “Whatever you do, don’t miss Queen Nefertiti in the Neues (New) Museum. Rumour has it that she’ll be returning to Egypt soon.”

The Nefertiti bust is the Museumsinsel’s most famous exhibit, a colourfully painted 19 inch tall limestone bust of the eponymous Egyptian queen, believed to have been created around 1340 BCE.

Called “an icon of international beauty” and even of “Berlin’s culture” since it was shipped to Germany in 1913, Germany has controversially consistently refused Egypt’s repeated demands for its repatriation. It is merely one example of how a murky colonial past generates moral and ethical dilemmas. To whose cultural heritage does a transplanted artefact, if taken forcibly (even if it was in keeping with the ‘laws’ of the time of the colonial power that took it), belong?

The Nefertiti bust was hermetically sealed in a glass case, and photography was prohibited, but we were encouraged to touch, feel and photograph a life-size replica displayed nearby.

The British Museum is notorious for possession of artefacts illegally taken from other countries (the Elgin marbles from Greece, the Benin Bronzes from modern-day Nigeria, and the Rosetta Stone from Egypt) and for its similar refusal of requests for their return. Its defense, that “the restitutionist premise, that whatever was made in a country must return to an original geographical site, would empty both the British Museum and the other great museums of the world" has been heavily criticized.

Another argument often made is that artefacts are safer and better-preserved in their present location than they would be if repatriated. But the British Museum itself is guilty of damage, for instance to the Elgin marbles, even if it was unintended.

In 2020, reacting to the Black Lives Matter movement around the world after the institutional murder of George Floyd in the US, the British Museum removed the bust of its founding father Sir Hans Sloane (1660 – 1753), as he was a slave-owner. Sloane (who has a square and other locations in London, and several plants and animals named after him) partly funded his collection from enslaved labour on Jamaican sugar plantations. His artefacts provided the nucleus for what became the British Museum.

A third argument, that artefacts should remain in the world’s famous museums because they are a repository where they can be viewed and savoured by millions more than if they were returned glosses over the fact that, for instance very few Nigerians have the opportunity to visit the Benin Bronzes, a chronicle of their heritage. “Taking them away is like yanking off pages of our history,” said a member of the Nigerian royal family. The bronzes were laid out in a very specific order, which has been scrambled and dispersed across the world.

Only a fraction of any museum’s collection is on display, the bulk residing in storage. I realized this first-hand. Prior to my recent visit to Vienna, I wrote to the city’s Kunsthistorisches (Art History) Museum. I wished to see a set of tapestries, titled ‘Deeds and Triumph of João de Castro’. They have a direct link to Goa as they depict events connected with the government of Portuguese India by D. João de Castro from 1495 to 1498.

I received no reply, but nevertheless went over to the museum’s Information and Help Desk when I arrived there. The employee was polite, but could not locate my email. So I showed it to him on my phone. He did a search and confirmed the items were in storage, but said storage items were scattered in different locations across Vienna, even beyond. So I never got the opportunity to see even one tapestry for myself.

This is not to say that I have become disillusioned with museums. It is just that as I get older, I still admire what I see, but I ask questions that may have escaped me before, when I was a wide-eyed younger adult.

When I see works in ivory, whale-bone, or rhinoceros horn, I think of the inevitable slaughter of those magnificent animals in their hundreds and thousands, all in the name of art.

When I admire a Chola bronze (incidentally displayed in such a cavalier manner at the Pondicherry state museum), I think of the anonymous ‘lower-caste’ master-sculptor responsible for these unique works of art. We know the deity, and can often trace it to a Chola ruler’s reign but the sculptor is unknown.

Erasure of Mughal and other periods of Indian history deemed “unreflective of Indian culture” are worrisome trends whether in renaming of streets, museums, or their disappearance from textbooks and school syllabi.

With the evolution of museology (study of museums), one hopes that museums everywhere will shift their focus to also create spaces for challenging conversations even if uncomfortable.

For instance, the University of Michigan Museum of Art houses a painting by Robert Thom from his 45-painting series ‘The History of Medicine’ (produced between 1948 and 1964), titled ‘J. Marion Sims: Gynecologic Surgeon.’

The painting ‘s purported ‘hero’ is Dr. Sims (1813 – 1883), black-coated, arms folded, as he and his white colleagues scrutinize Lucy, an enslaved woman with the complication of obstructed labour, vesicovaginal fistula, an abnormal passage between the bladder and the vagina. Thom depicts her fully clothed, but she would have been examined stripped naked, an indignity that would never have been inflicted on white women.

The original accompanying text by George A. Bender eulogized Sims as “a leader in gynaecology in Europe as well as in the United States.” Indeed, until recently, Sims was lauded as “the father of modern gynaecology.” As an obstetrician and gynaecologist myself, this is how my textbooks presented him to me.

But after Sims has been outed for his racism, butchery, and cruelty in the name of surgical innovation, an added text speaks of the agonizing pain suffered by his patients as he operated on them without anesthesia, and that Sims “also failed to acknowledge the fact that the enslaved female patients were expected to continue their servitude even as patients. These women not only cooked, cleaned, and cared for the Sims family, but effectively kept the Alabama hospital running, serving as Sim’s surgical nurses throughout the five-year period of their ‘treatment’.”

It ends thus: “In reconsidering the captions for these images, we ask: how can we use this knowledge to make new and informed assessments about Thom’s visual panoramas and Bender’s accompanying texts? How can we negotiate the allure of the artwork’s narrative in ‘Great Moments in Medicine’ while also acknowledging its charged nature?”

The same painting, once seen as ‘heroic’, now poses very different questions in light of the new information about the once-perceived ‘hero.’ Rather than remove the painting, the museum uses it as a starting point into the ethics of medical research and advances, slavery, and much more.

And this should continue to be the purpose of museums in the 21st century: to educate and challenge us to think about our past, and use those lessons for a better future.

Dr. Luis Dias is a physician, musician and founder of Child's Play India Foundation (www.childsplayindia.org), a music education trust that provides classical music education to India’s underprivileged and disadvantaged children. He has a keen interest in history and leads heritage walks in Goa. He archives his writings at www.luisdias.wordpress.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Luis Dias
Moving through London, Berlin, Vienna, and India, Dr. Luis Dias observed how museums housed masterpieces as well as stole histories and erased artisans. He writes about how museums can become places for uncomfortable but necessary conversations.

item.title: Museums of the everyday
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Usha Raman

Many homes, in many countries across the world, have a space that is meant for display, or perhaps an entire room that is laid out in very carefully curated ways to showcase taste, memory, heritage, and occasionally, achievement. From glass cabinets to corner shelves to objects arranged on tables and pictures on the wall, these displays speak of lives lived, aspirations or their fulfilment, and create for both the resident and the visitor a certain sense of identity. Every conscious act of home decoration is a form of expression, and says something about how one views oneself or would like to be viewed. It’s part of the story we tell about ourselves. Right from the welcome mat on the floor or the toran on the door frame, we announce a belief system, or an aesthetic direction – is it coir or rubber, is the pattern floral or geometric, are there mango leaves and flowers over the door, or a delicately constructed beaded design? And then, when you walk inside, do you see family photographs and sports trophies, or a variety of foreign souvenirs, or paintings? Is there a space for books, and what kind are they?

It’s a landscape that is sometimes strategically curated, at others, just casually assembled, but either way, it is rich with information, available to read if we wish.

Thinking about how we set up our homes and look at the way familiar objects are positioned can be a useful entry to get children to recognize how curation works at different levels, as well as to develop an appreciation of the relationships between deceptively simple, everyday products and processes and the broader cultures we live in.

Of course, things are never as simple as they seem, nor are they always the result of deliberate thought and action. All spaces and the things within them involve expenditure of some kind, and all decisions reflect some power structures, but such dynamics can also be uncovered by looking carefully, listening attentively, and reflecting on what we see and hear.

Activity (Middle school)

Look, Ask, Listen, Think: Ask the children to go back home and observe the different spaces. They may have a room or a space that is meant for receiving visitors, or an exterior section that is decorated in specific ways. Beyond the functional areas (seating arrangements, work spaces), what else do they see? Are there objects that are there only for display? They can ask their parents or other decision makers in the home about why certain things were placed on the wall or on shelves. What is the story being told by these objects – singly and as a collection?

In the living room of my home (what some call a “drawing room”), for instance, I have two small shelves in two corners, on each of which I’ve arranged some objects. One corner has a set of small brass figurines – a Ganesha, a dancing Nataraja, a baby Krishna, Muruga with a peacock behind him, and a wooden replica of the Buddha from Anuradhapura in Sri Lanka. The other corner has two “tin can animals” and a stone sculpture from Zimbabwe, and a set of small hand painted pottery from Anatolia in Turkey. There’s intention there, and a theme. One side has things from my culture, while the other has things from abroad, representing my memory of travel. It’s possible that your homes contain such corners too, and that you have seen such collections in other homes.

Some home displays are more deliberately curated, and can end up becoming significant as they build a history of some kind. In Secunderabad, collector Yenugu Krishnamurthy[*] has assembled an impressive array of not-so-ordinary objects, from brass pots to copper plates to musical instruments, that, one journalist observes, represents an “old-school vibe”. What started with two old brass pots from his grandmother grew into a heritage collection. Another friend of mine began buying unusual teapots from places she visited, and before she knew it, had a collection of more than 30 beautiful teapots that sit on a specially built upper rack that runs on four sides of a room. As children, we collect stamps, coins, and rocks, and press leaves and flowers, and depending on the level of interest, organize them in ways that allow us to label, annotate, and, space permitting, even display them.

The instinct to acquire, organize, and display is not confined just to formal museums. Some of us do the first two as a private activity, collecting things from bookmarks and pens to magnets and keychains. Souvenir shops in tourist locations exploit this tendency to buy keepsakes, selling us a variety of sundry objects that serve no purpose other than to remind us of the good times we had in that place. My refrigerator is now groaning under the weight of magnets from my own travels and a few from other people who know I love them. It’s my own museum of memory.

Activity (middle to high school)

Ask your class to think about the word ‘memorabilia’, and after a short discussion, assign the following task(s). Do you have objects that are kept specifically because they remind you of a place, person, or time? Put them together and take a picture (or more, if needed) that we can call a memory frame. Write short labels for each, much like the descriptions you see on a museum display, mentioning not only what the object is but why it is in the collection. Create a display wall in the classroom for a jointly assembled “museum of memorabilia”. Briefly speak about your display.

One interesting museum I visited was organized in a way that told a story. In fact, it was a story-turned-into-a-display. Nobel Laureate Orhan Pamuk’s Museum of Innocence is a book written like a family memoir, full of details about holiday trips, youthful romance and heartbreak, large gatherings where food and wine were plentiful, politics on a large and small scale. Imagine now that this is turned into a museum, with pivotal moments and locales from the book displayed in different ways. Visiting the physical Museum of Innocence in Istanbul was like walking through the pages of the book, with recognizable objects (a piano, children’s toys, a sofa) from the story reminding the visitor of events from the story.

Museums are an expression of the human impulses to hold on to memory on the one hand, and to fashion perception, on the other. They contain objects that allow us to freeze time and examine them closely, sometimes resulting in varying interpretations as we understand contexts and circumstances differently over time. But they can also be screens through which we filter out and in – how we want to be seen, and how we see the world. This is why, at societal and national levels, museums can be contentious spaces, regulating how (and what) history is constructed and viewed. But these dynamics become clear even at the micro level, within our homes, as we first collect, then carefully choose, the objects which speak for us, and show not only who we are, but how we – as individuals, families, communities – relate to the world.

[*] See: https://ykantiques.com
item.excerpt:encoded:
Usha Raman
Homes often hold curated corners with family photographs, travel souvenirs, and inherited objects that say as much about us as any formal collection. Usha Raman writes about how these everyday objects help us hold on to memory and shape how we see – and wish to be seen.

item.title: The wondrous power of objects
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sananda Mukhopadhyaya

A museum is, above all, a place of curiosity, a place where wonder lives. And yet wonder, that powerful human experience that sparks imagination, decisions, and even lifelong pursuits, often finds little room in our school curricula. We hope children will wonder, but we do not teach them how. We ask questions and build pathways for inquiry, but it is only when we step outside the classroom that true wonder can unfold.

A visit to a museum can have a lasting effect on a child’s imagination. In the quiet darkness, when an object is lit on a pedestal, wonder thrives. Here, a child is drawn not only to the object itself or to the text that accompanies it, but to the obscure, deeply human stories that lie beyond the pages of their textbooks or the conversations they have at home. In this setting, the child is free to think independently, to question, connect, and even to disagree. Because an object on display is not an instruction, it is an invitation. It does not dictate meaning; it simply exists, carrying with it an unspoken trust that it matters.

But what if we could bring this experience into the classroom? Imagine a classroom transformed into a small museum, where each student brings an object from home that holds personal or familial significance, perhaps something belonging to an elder who once undertook a maritime journey or something a parent or caregiver uses in their job. Once placed on a pedestal, this object becomes an artefact. With a small note describing its story and maybe even facts about its material composition, the child connects their personal world to something larger, something human, and even something within the curriculum. Suddenly, learning becomes both deeply personal and profoundly meaningful.

Our classrooms often acknowledge prior knowledge, but what about prior experience? What about the stories, values, and inherited wisdom that shape who we are? Personal histories have little place in our classrooms, but they must. They should be integral to everyday learning.

In India, though the museum is no longer a new concept, its place in our learning systems remains undervalued. Perhaps this is because we were never really a people of objects; we were a people of stories. Especially those passed through generations. And of course, within these narratives, there would be some stories attached to objects too.

I grew up with a grandmother who was displaced in the city and whose memories lived in a single trunk that I would later inherit. None of her possessions was conventionally precious, yet each held a story. One such story was of a miniature kitchen utensil made in marble, which her father had brought back from a visit to Kashi. A place that would occupy a large portion of her imagination as a child, a place she would later visit with her mother after her father had passed. I grew up seeing a photo of her mother standing next to a river in Kashi, set in a small silver frame that sat at her altar. All her objects told me a story of her yearning to return home. She was very stern with me, and it was through her objects and their stories that I learned to see her softer sides. But it was a trunk that would appear in a photograph in my grade 10 history textbook, of people moving during the partition, that would really leave an impression on me. Up until then, my grandmother's objects didn't hold as much meaning for me; it was then, in that moment that I understood what it truly meant to yearn for a home you cannot return to.

I am sure everyone I went to school with had a version of such stories, tied to moments of loss and resilience. But our classrooms had never made space for such sharing.

When classrooms make space, even briefly, for students to share their personal narratives in a safe environment, they cultivate authentic empathy and a sense of belonging. This form of engagement moves beyond performative exercises, embedding empathy through lived experience rather than prescription.

Learning that begins with emotional engagement becomes deeper, more durable, and more inclusive. When a learner connects emotionally with an object or story, understanding moves beyond cognition; it becomes lived and felt. Museum-based learning, rooted in encounters with objects, invites this kind of engagement. Objects speak across language, background, and ability; they allow every learner to enter the conversation from their own experience. In this way, learning through objects not only deepens comprehension but also democratizes it, making the classroom a more inclusive and empathetic place.

For example, if I were to teach water conservation, I would bring a steel pot into the classroom and tell the story of women in Marathwada who walk 2 to 3 kilometers every day to collect water for their families. With older students, I might explore how this reality shapes social structures, even leading to practices like polygamy, born from the sheer need for more hands to fetch water. That one steel pot, resting in the classroom museum all week, would transform an abstract lesson into lived empathy. Each glance at it would recall a story, a struggle, a people, and through that, ignite compassion and understanding.

The power of looking at a single object in isolation, and yet in connection with an entire history, is often underestimated in education. We still trust words more than things. But sometimes, it is an object – humble, tangible, ordinary – that can move us most profoundly. Objects have the ability to stir emotion. They touch something deeply human, activating empathy and connection. When this happens collectively, it strengthens a sense of community within the classroom. Learning ceases to be an individual act; it becomes shared, felt, and remembered. Emotional connection hardwires understanding; it helps knowledge endure because it is no longer abstract, but embodied.

Objects demand reverence. In museums, this reverence is heightened through lighting, display, and silence. And it can be recreated in classrooms too. The ritual, the stillness, the performance of attention are not decoration; they are tools of immersion and imagination. Children naturally understand this; it’s why they treat their toys with such devotion!

We think of museum objects as still, but it is the experiences we build around them that make them come alive. The power of wonder must be trusted. It is what drives true learning and change. It is what propels the human experience forward. In an age where all information is instantly accessible, the role of the teacher is to make the classroom a place of embodied learning – a kind of temple. And what is a museum, after all, if not a temple, a place where we feel part of a whole?

Sananda Mukhopadhyaya is a theatre maker and art-based educator. She studied at St.Xavier’s and Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahlaya/CSMVS in Mumbai. She currently works at the American School of Bombay as the curator of the India Sanskriti Center. She can be reached at sananda.mukho@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sananda Mukhopadhyaya
What happens when we treat everyday objects as invitations to wonder? Sananda Mukhopadhyaya writes about museum-based learning and how it can help children connect to their individual and combined histories in lasting ways.

item.title: An immersive musical walkthrough
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Sadhana Ramchander

We visited the Indian Music Experience (IME) Museum in May 2022, after the pandemic eased a little. This trip to Bengaluru was the first we made after being confined to the house like everyone around the world. During this frustrating and scary chapter of our lives, one reassuring constant was music. Whether it was film songs, classical music, western pop, or jazz, it was a time when music, as it always does, gave us comfort and calm. But there was a real worry about the world. About the people who made music. Live performances came to an abrupt stop. Musicians stayed home, and many of them posted clips of their music online. There was frustration, anger, and desperation.

The world slowly opened up, and grateful to be able to travel again, we were in Bengaluru, and on the beautiful Brigade Millennium Avenue, where IME is located.

As one enters the museum, on a blue wall in white lettering, are these words:

“We hear sounds of nature, in the chorus of crickets, the beating of the heart, and the pitter-patter of rain. These sounds are the building blocks of the universal language of melody and rhythm. The stories of our lives find expression in songs which celebrate all that we hold sacred.”

This sets the tone for what comes next.

A couple of kitschy and colourful autorickshaws greet you, and you realize they are part of a recreated Commercial Street, an old and bustling market place in Bengaluru. Inside the autos are video panels on which a documentary is shown in one and concerts on a loop in the other. Then your gaze falls on a display of posters of Indie bands such as Indian Ocean, Millennium, Pentagram, Beat Bands, and Rock Machine. One display is of Daler Mehendi’s long colourful jacket with information about what musicians wear. You then come face-to-face with the "Wall of Manganiyars" – a spectacular, immersive stage setup used in the theatrical concert, The Manganiyar Seduction, created by director Roysten Abel, where the performers sit in a tiered, glowing wall of individual boxes.

We are already happy and eager to discover more!

The museum has many informative and interactive exhibits about various genres of music such as folk, classical, contemporary, and even songs from the independence movement in a section called ‘Songs of struggle’. A letter from Mahatma Gandhi to M S Subbulakshmi, blessing her for her good work with raising funds for the Kasturba Memorial Fund, finds a place here. A section is devoted to the origins of music –beginning with the sacred ‘Om’ chant and the ritual chants of the Sama Veda.

A beautiful chart – Samay chakra – lists out Hindustani raagas for various times of the day – Pre-dawn – Sohini and Paraj, dawn – Bhatiyar and Lalit; early morning – Bhairavi, Ramkali, and Jogia, and so on. This is based on the traditional idea of the relationship between the environment and people’s moods, and how musical notes can evoke an emotional or spiritual response. An attractive display of the various gharanas with photos of the musician they are attributed to is another aspect of the Hindustani music section. A huge poster gives information on how the harmonium came to India in the mid-19th century and became an integral part of Hindustani music, replacing the Sarangi.

Among many displays and interactive kiosks is one called Sounds of Excellence. One can listen to torchbearers such as M D Ramanathan, G N Balasubramaniam, Voleti Venkateshwarlu, Emani Shankara Sastry, U Srinivas, and so on.

Bollywood and its music are very much part of the museum. The charming Raj Kapoor and Nargis under an umbrella stand tall at the beginning of a ‘Stories through song’ section. You can discover the legends of Hindi film music and the historic narrative since the 1930s; listen to your favourite songs, and take selfies and photos. The butt chairs from Three Idiots are amusing and you can get a photo sitting alongside Amir Khan et al!

Stories within stories

The museum is really like a labyrinth and has stories within stories within stories. To package the evolution of music in India is not an easy task. It is not just the various genres of music per se, but includes musical instruments and the media through which one has listened to music since the 1920s.

A visually striking Instruments Gallery from floor to ceiling in black and red décor displays various kinds of musical instruments. More than a 100 string, wind, percussion, and other kinds of instruments are displayed. The plaque here reads, “The abundance of musical instruments in Indian music is testament to its diversity. Each one is unique, not only for its sound, but also for its craftsmanship.” As such, the instruments on display are very aesthetic, and one doesn’t have to go far to listen to the music each one makes at the various kiosks placed there.

The evolution of music players is also interesting and historical. From radio to gramophone records to large round spools on massive players that were the precursors to cassettes, and CDs, and DVDs…and how over the years, poof! all these media disappeared. Cassette and CD/DVD shops closed down, and we just had online music that we had to figure out how to listen to. Music itself changed from being something a family listened to together, to a very personalized experience with headphones or earpods. Large music systems gave way to smaller ones, and now there are almost no music players any more. A two-in-one was the easiest thing to buy, in order to listen to all the cassettes and CDs one had collected, but not any more. They are simply not available, and the old machines somehow do not work, as though they know that they have been replaced with the next generation of music enablers.

The Music Experience Museum traces this evolution with displays of radios from the 1920s, gramophones, cassette/CD players, and televisions on the walls, with detailed information about their evolution over the decades. They also have records, cassettes, and CDs displayed artistically on the wall, making one wonder if, in these digital times, that is the only use for the ones in people’s homes too.

A rooftop amphitheatre is another feature of the museum and is designed for performances, as also a small recording studio where you can record your voice for karaoke songs. An interactive exhibit allows visitors to find their pitch by singing into a computer. What’s more, their pitch is matched with that of different artistes, making the whole experience thrilling!

Every bit of the museum is a treat for the music lover!

Exhibitions

The museum has held seven exhibitions (offline and online) till date, according to their website. The day we visited, there was an exhibition titled, “Birds in Indian Music”. Curated by IME, this exhibition highlighted the contribution birds make to ecology and culture. One could see a display of musical instruments inspired by birds, breathtaking photographs and illustrations, listen to bird calls on audio-visual kiosks, the emphasis being on the co-existence between birds and humans in these days of climate change.

I was excited to learn the names of classical raagas named after birds, such as Kokilavarali, Hamsa Nandini, Kokilapriya, Balahamsa, Garudadhwani, Kokilabhashini, Hamsadhwani (Carnatic), and in Hindustani – Hansakinkini and Vihang (meaning bird). These raagas do not necessarily have a direct connection to the birds; in Carnatic music, they refer to mounts or vaahanas of gods and goddesses.

Says a friend and fellow naturalist Arpita Joshi, “I saw the special Birdsong exhibit with a friend and her two sons. 'The Earth has music for those who will listen' it said right at the entrance. The exhibit brought not just the mechanics of Birdsong to us but the beauty, variation, myth and magic of birds, highlighting the dangers of dwindling birds and birdsong, especially in urban spaces. It was an uplifting, soulful and thought-provoking space.”

Everyone loves IME!

There are great reviews online, and high praise for the museum from everyone who goes there. “I loved the Indian Music Experience Museum,” says Malavika Ramchander, an artist and connoisseur of music. “It presents several ways in which one can interact with the exhibits, which is so exciting. The museum cannot be covered in a single visit and requires several visits,” she says.

Vibheeshan K V, another connoisseur, says, “I really enjoyed visiting the museum. There is a lot to learn because of the many genres of music that one can experience here.”

Says Arpita Joshi, “I am reminded of a particularly memorable half-day spent with a friend there during the pandemic when we ended up having the museum to ourselves. We listened/interacted with all their exhibits to our hearts content, sang along to the music we grew up with, discovered much and ended the visit by recording ourselves in the karaoke booth. What a much-required escape from the constant weight of the pandemic that was!”

The impressive building that houses IME was designed by Architecture Paradigm from Bengaluru. It takes the curvilinear shape of the trees on site, and the flowing nature of Indian music – ideas that took my breath away! The world-renowned Gallagher & Associates, who designed the Grammy Museum in Los Angeles and the Woodstock Museum in New York, also designed the exhibits at IME.

The presentation, aesthetics, depth of content, technology used, high fidelity headphones, and attention to detail all contribute to making this museum meet international standards. We definitely need many such spaces of brilliance; in fact, every state in India deserves one to preserve and exhibit regional, contemporary, folk, and tribal music from various regions.

On the way out, you can spend time at the Sound Garden and visit the interesting Museum Shop where you can enjoy looking at some well-designed creatives and pick up a souvenir or two.

Sadhana Ramchander is a Consultant Editor/Writer, who also works to connect children and adults with Nature. She can be reached at sadhana.ramchander@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Sadhana Ramchander
From the Samay chakra’s raagas for every hour to the visual thrill of the Manganiyars’ glowing stage, Sadhana Ramchander writes how the Indian Music Experience in Bengaluru maps India’s musical soul.

item.title: How a field trip birthed a living museum
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Divya Shukla

Ayodhya has always fascinated me. At the age of 20, I explored this ancient city as a young journalist, my eyes filled with curiosity, my notebook filled with questions. I remember walking along the ghats and winding alleys, chasing stories that whispered from the walls. Back then, I was chasing fragments of the past. I didn’t know that, decades later, I would be called back not as a seeker, but as a storyteller, guiding students into the very heart of history.

Now, as a geography teacher at JB Academy, I see the world through a different lens, but that dormant seed of historical inquiry was still buried somewhere inside me. It took my students their energy, restlessness, and longing for real-world learning to water that seed and bring it to life.

One winter morning, everything changed.

I stood before my class, watching my students yearn for something beyond textbook learning. Their eagerness sparked something inside me. “Let’s explore Ayodhya,” I said spontaneously, my voice barely containing my excitement. The response was instant and overwhelming. In that moment, I could feel it: something extraordinary was about to begin.

The day that changed everything

On the chilly morning of December 25, 2022, we set off. Forty spirited students, none trained in archaeology but all united by unfiltered enthusiasm, boarded a bus with kitchen tools, garden tools, and brushes in hand. Our destination: Raj Ghat, a site I had long admired for its remarkable geological structure, an enormous mud wall that acts like a natural time machine. As you climb upward, you walk through more recent layers of time. But the deeper you dig, the older the earth becomes, until eventually, you find yourself in ancient history.

It was here that my 40 amateur explorers began their work. At first, there was hesitation. Then came wonder.

“Ma’am, look at this! It’s so smooth and black” one student cried, holding a shard of northern black polished ware.

What followed was beyond even my dreams. In one long day, these young explorers uncovered over 300 artefacts – terracotta shards, ancient clay beads, miniature toys, fragments of walls and statues, and even 24 cm-long, 5 mm-thick clay glasses. I remember one boy who, with intense focus, balanced himself for over three hours on a ledge to extract one such buried glass. That moment wasn’t about the object itself, it was about the ownership he felt, the discovery that belonged to him.

The site we explored was an abandoned stretch along the Saryu Ghat, its landscape shaped and scarred by years of river erosion. Once a thriving settlement, the area still bears the silent footprints of its past – weathered remains that speak of a time when life here flourished. Over the years, the shifting course of the Saryu has brought frequent floods, forcing residents to abandon their homes. Even today, the land remains vulnerable to inundation.

Our visit took place in December, during the post-monsoon lull, when the site lay calm and accessible. It was a deeply enriching experience, offering both discovery and reflection. Every detail was documented exactly as it appeared; not a single centimeter of earth was disturbed in the process of collecting artefacts. The exploration was carried out under the careful supervision of Dr. Deshraj Upadhyaya, Museum In-charge at Dr. RML Awadh University, Ayodhya, in collaboration with INTACH, Ayodhya Chapter – a dedicated force in heritage conservation. Adding to the significance of the day, our museum welcomed the Director of the Allahabad Museum and his team, whose visit lent further recognition to our efforts.

We carefully wrapped each artefact in old newspapers. It was dusk by the time we left Raj Ghat, exhausted but exhilarated. That evening was not the close of an exploration, it was the dawn of a movement. The idea of a school museum was no longer a dream. It was now an urgent necessity.

Back at school, the buzz was contagious. Students from other classes peeped into our storage room, excitedly whispering about the findings. But my Super 40 were already asking, “What next?” I was asking the same question myself.

With immense gratitude, I recall the support we received from our Director, Ms. Manjula Jhunjhunwala. In a casual yet inspiring interaction, the idea of a museum surfaced. Her encouragement gave our effort direction and shape.

What began as a geography class soon transformed into a living experiment in history, archaeology, project management, and community building. Every day, we spent the first 15 minutes of my period planning, discussing, and designing the museum. Mr. Deshraj Upadhyaya and Ms. Aishwarya Srivastav, an expert in archaeological practice, became our mentors.

Our classroom turned into a workshop. Students learned how to clean centuries-old pottery, identify typologies, and assign catalog numbers. The mentors taught the students how to measure circular objects using thread and rulers from their geometry kits applying mathematics to history in a way no textbook could teach.

When regular classes began to leave us with too little time, the students made a bold decision: they would work on holidays. That’s when I knew we were no longer running a school project we were nurturing a revolution in experiential education.

Building the school museum

The next challenge was space and infrastructure. We needed display cases, but our budget was minimal. The management offered unused, discarded furniture from various departments, old and worn out, but salvageable. I got to work designing museum-specific fixtures from these odds and ends, and our school carpenter, bless his craftsmanship, executed them beautifully. For just Rs. 10,000, we created custom-coded almirahs and showcases, repurposing with purpose.

One classroom was allotted for the museum. Piece by piece, the space took shape. Students cleaned, measured, and photographed every artefact. They entered data, debated layouts, and created digital archives. After two months of tireless work, the documentation was complete. Our academic year ended with something far more enduring than report cards: we had built the first school-based student-run museum in Ayodhya.

As my Super 40 entered the demanding class 12 phase, we needed a new batch to carry the torch. This second group didn’t just inherit a museum, they inherited a mission. In handover sessions, the original team trained their juniors not only in technical tasks but also in philosophy. The challenge now was not to build, but to curate. They learned the power of storytelling: how each object must speak, how labels and arrangement can shape understanding.

Their biggest test came when they had to present the museum’s story to the school leadership. They delivered with clarity, confidence, and poise. Watching them speak, I was reassured: our legacy was in safe hands.

The living museum: a community awakens

ETHNO HISTORICA soon outgrew its physical space and its original vision.

As news spread, the community stepped forward. Parents, teachers, students, helping staff, grandparents, neighbours brought in relics of the past: a Gandhian charkha, vintage paan dans, gulab pash, chausar with silver pawns, fossils ammonite, a huge clay pot, a white collar with fine embroidery worn by British women, old wooden chess, wooden and metal boxes, pre-independence coins, rare textiles with Sindhi embroidery, an 18-kg iron vintage fan, and many more. But these weren’t just donations; they came with stories. And those stories became part of our collection.

Now our students were oral historians, interviewing donors, recording their memories, preserving not just objects, but emotions. The museum evolved into a platform for cultural dialogue, heritage preservation, and civic pride.

Students organized museum days, designed quizzes and exhibitions, created campaigns to promote awareness. They were learning event management, leadership, and digital marketing skills no textbook teaches.

With time, our little classroom couldn’t contain us. Recognizing the growing impact of the initiative, J B Academy’s management offered us a larger space. It was the ultimate affirmation: the school believed in this as much as we did.

As I walk through the museum today, past shelves of carefully labelled artefacts and placards, I feel an overwhelming sense of gratitude. This project began with one spontaneous suggestion. It became a transformative journey for every student involved, including myself.

From a journalist chasing stories to a teacher helping students create stories, I’ve come full circle. ETHNO HISTORICA is not just a museum; it is living proof that the richest learning happens when we step beyond the classroom and listen to the world beneath our feet.

Education, I’ve learned, isn’t always about answers. Sometimes, it’s about questions; It’s about wonder, curiosity, community, and connection. ETHNO HISTORICA taught us that history isn’t confined to textbooks or lecture halls. It lives in attics, in memory, in the hands of our elders, and in the soil beneath our feet. Ayodhya gave us its past. Through this museum, we give it a future.

Dr. Divya Shukla is an educator and heritage enthusiast at J B Academy, Ayodhya. She is the founder-mentor of ETHNO HISTORICA, a student-led museum initiative that explores the convergence of culture, education, and community engagement. She can be reached atdivya743@jbnss.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Divya Shukla
A spontaneous field trip to Ayodhya becomes a turning point for a teacher and her students. Dr Divya Shukla shares how one day of discovery at Raj Ghat led to over 300 artifacts, a movement in experiential learning, and the inauguration of a student-run living museum.

item.title: The museum between worlds
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Divya Choudary

To step into a museum is to enter an interface – a design between worlds. One made of matter – stone, wood, cloth, porcelain; the other, of memory, imagination, and light. Every display case, label, and beam of light is part of a quiet architecture that helps us move between what is there and what is gone.

Museums have always been mediators between the tangible and the invisible. They are built to preserve what endures, but in doing so, they reveal what doesn’t. The chipped sculpture, the faded miniature, the ritual object once alive with sound or scent – all hold the trace of a living rhythm. A museum is a choreography of presences and absences, arranged with care. What we see is not just preservation, but interruption – an artefact removed from the flow of life that gave it meaning.

Some museums understand this as experience. The space becomes a kind of user interface, with light, silence, and sequence guiding us through the unseen story beneath the objects. Others can feel more like databases, rows of things behind glass, rich in inventory but sparse in atmosphere. Both conserve, but only one allows for connection. Yet, the desire endures – to know how these objects lived before they were labelled. What might it mean to experience a clock not as an exhibit, but within the room it once marked time for? A thali not as brass under glass, but as a meal once shared?

Now, as museums welcome a new kind of intangible – the virtual – this interface expands rather than replaces stone and glass. Where the earlier intangible was what had disappeared, this new kind becomes a way to bring absence into being. Virtual museums, digital experiences, and augmented reality extend our reach, letting the tactile and the virtual begin to share a boundary – two layers of design, stacked one upon another.

Perhaps what draws us back to museums, again and again, is this quiet longing to cross time, to stand before what has survived and feel, even briefly, the pulse of what hasn’t. Every visit is a kind of correspondence across centuries; we send our gaze forward, and something from the past looks back. In that act of exchange, the boundaries of time blur. Digital tools, when used with care, do not diminish that encounter; they extend it. They allow us to meet the object halfway, to imagine how it once moved, sounded, or was held, how it was used by the people of its time, the place it occupied in their homes, and the stories and rituals it once witnessed.

Across India, museums are beginning to explore new ways of crossing time. At Hyderabad’s Salar Jung Museum, an audio guide app now lets visitors walk through its vast collections at their own pace, carrying the museum’s voice in their pocket. Scanning a QR code, a visitor hears the clock share its story – its maker, its echo of another century. The miniature paintings whisper of their past, the carved ivory speaks of its journey. For a collection as vast and varied as Salar Jung’s, this simple act of narration transforms how one walks through it. Rows of labelled objects become corridors of voices; the act of looking becomes an act of listening; the artefact a narrator.

In Delhi, the National Museum – soon to evolve into the Yuge Yugeen Bharat Museum – continues to extend the same idea through sight, in its Virtual Galleries. Visitors can explore 3D models of sculptures and relics – from Ramayana miniatures to Buddhist art, Company paintings, and gold ornaments from Takshashila – without ever stepping into the museum’s halls. It isn’t just convenience; it’s another kind of time travel, a way of entering history without disturbing its stillness.

At the Museum of Art & Photography (MAP) in Bengaluru, this dialogue takes on a new dimension. Using voice, projection, and artificial intelligence, MAP has created a 3D hologram of the artist M.F. Husain. Visitors can ask questions and hear his simulated response, an echo of a voice crafted through code. Though the “AI” element follows a script rather than true conversation, the effect remains striking. To hear a familiar voice reply, even through a reconstruction, is to sense how technology can bridge silence with story. MAP’s approach suggests a future where museums may not only display art, but also speak with it; where dialogue, not display, becomes the heart of the experience. For a moment, absence feels less absolute.

These efforts link into a broader foundation, the National Digital Repository for Museums of India, which gathers collections from across the country into a single shared digital home. Thousands of artefacts, once separated by geography, now coexist in one searchable space, accessible to anyone, anywhere, at any time. It may not be as sensorial as VR, but its quiet power lies in reach. It extends across distance, turning preservation into participation.

Across the world, similar experiments are unfolding. If some museums are learning to go virtually where time once stopped, others are going where the body cannot, turning preservation into access.

At the Vizcaya Museum and Gardens in Miami – once a home – digital tools now open spaces otherwise largely inaccessible. Through Virtual Vizcaya, visitors can wander the Barge, a stone ship moored in the bay, and the Swimming Pool Grotto, both fragile, both closed to the public. Here, digital photogrammetry and 3D models do, virtually, what physical conservation struggles to achieve – allowing visitors to experience places that time and tide have begun to reclaim. And in Yours Truly, a mobile virtual experience, Vizcaya reconstructs its founder’s world through letters, telegrams, and 3D objects – a way of witnessing a life, not just a house.

Together, these initiatives – storytelling, simulation, digital archiving, and AR experiments – remind us that museums have always been augmented realities. The glass case is a technology. The spotlight, the caption, and the curatorial voice are all forms of mediation. Yet, the impulse remains the same – to let technology extend the reach of care. The museum becomes less a room of relics and more a living interface between touch and memory, matter and light. The digital merely makes that mediation visible. It reveals what has always been true – that seeing is never simple. It means navigating between touch and distance, between fact and feeling, between what can be held and what must be imagined.

The future of the museum may not be about spectacle, but subtlety – about designing experiences that let the tangible and the intangible coexist without cancelling each other out. It asks us to rethink what it means to visit, to move not only through rooms, but through layers of presence. When the screen leans in beside the statue, when code meets clay, something quiet happens.

We begin to “look” differently.

Each digital replica resists forgetting, even as it reminds us of how much we stand to lose. The museum’s turn toward code and cloud is not just progress; it is a kind of plea. We digitize because we dread disappearance. We replicate because we cannot return. Because the museum has never really been about what it holds, but about how it helps us hold on. Between material and memory, object and story, absence and presence – it is, and will continue to be, an interface for wonder.

Explore further:

National Museum’s Virtual Galleries: https://nmvirtual.in/

National Digital Repository for Museums: https://museumsofindia.gov.in/repository

The Museum of Art & Photography Bengaluru: https://map-india.org/the-ai-husain-experience/

Vizcaya Museum and Gardens: https://tour.virtualvizcaya.org/ and https://vizcaya.org/yourstruly/

Salar Jung Museum: https://salarjungmuseum.in/

Divya Choudary works with narrative and design in edtech, drawing on her background in print and new media and children’s literature and literacies. She enjoys exploring storyworlds across books and various media, always with a cup of tea in hand. She can be reached at dchoudary@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Divya Choudary
Museums are interfaces between matter and memory, helping us move between what survives and what has vanished. Divya Choudary writes about how light, silence, narration, and now digital tools deepen this dialogue between presence and absence.

item.title: Museums as living classrooms: exploring English beyond textbooks
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Aparna D

When we think of a museum, we often imagine neatly labelled objects that belong to another time, locked in glass cases. But museums are much more than that. They tell stories of people, emotions, and cultures. Each artefact carries a voice from the past, whispering experiences that shaped who we are.

As English teachers, we can turn these quiet spaces into vibrant learning grounds. A visit to a museum – or even a virtual exploration – can open new possibilities to teach stories, emotions, and language in a meaningful way.
