Museums help us go beyond the printed page and bring literature to life through experiences that students can observe, interpret, and reflect upon.

English lessons and museums – a natural link

Literature, like museums, captures human experience. Both require observation interpretation, and understanding. When students step into a museum, they enter a world where language, art, and history come together. It allows them to connect what they read in their English textbooks with what they see before them.

For instance, an art museum can inspire creative writing, while a history museum can provide a real backdrop to poems or prose that reflect social change, courage, or personal struggle. Even a small school museum or virtual gallery can offer the same experience, encouraging students to look beyond words and explore meanings through visuals and objects.

Lessons from classes 9 to 12 that can be connected with museum experiences

Class 9 – Exploring culture and place (Beehive / Moments)

The poems and stories in class 9 are filled with emotion, imagination, and reflection. They speak of Nature, culture, and relationships. Students can write ekphrastic poems – poems inspired by an artwork or object – drawing from what they see and feel, building observation and expression naturally.

Similarly, Kathmandu by Vikram Seth offers a unique opportunity to explore journeys, diversity, and spirituality through cultural artefacts. Students can virtually explore exhibits from the Indian Museum Kolkata, or the Chhatrapati Shivaji Maharaj Vastu Sangrahalaya in Mumbai, which feature South Asian art, temple architecture, and traditional attire. They can design a museum-style display titled “Two Cities, Two Worlds”, comparing Kathmandu and Banaras through visuals, postcards, and symbolic objects such as prayer wheels, temple bells, and ghats.

Class 10 – Learning from lives and voices (First Flight / Footprints without Feet)

The chapters Nelson Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom and From the Diary of Anne Frank offer rich opportunities to connect literature with history and human experience. In my classes, I regularly collaborate with the social science mentor to set the historical context behind these lessons. Together, we help students explore the realities of apartheid and Holocaust, discuss struggles, courage, and resilience and reflect on the broader significance of human rights and empathy.

A museum visit – or even a virtual exploration of the Apartheid Museum (South Africa) or the Anne Frank House (Amsterdam) – can enhance this understanding. Students can study artefacts, photographs, and letters to visualize the stories and write reflective pieces such as “The Object that Told Mandela’s Story” or “Anne Frank’s World Through Artefacts.”

Class 11 – Reading the past through artefacts (Hornbill: Discovering Tut – The Saga Continues)

This lesson almost reads like a museum catalogue. Students can step into the shoes of archaeologists and historians acting as junior curators to select objects or images from virtual museums and write museum-style captions linking them to the text. Such tasks encourage inquiry, interpretation, and critical thinking.

Class 12 – Interpreting society and change (Flamingo / Vistas)

Themes in Indigo, Lost Spring, and The Last Lesson can be linked to social history and heritage museums. Observing exhibits on freedom movements, child labour, or cultural transitions, help students see the deeper human stories behind the texts. They can prepare essays, posters, or small exhibits connecting what they read with what they observe.

Developing language skills through museum activities

Museum-based learning builds language skills naturally. When students describe artefacts, interpret exhibits, or prepare short presentations, they use rich vocabulary and expressions. Observation and reflection enhance writing and speaking fluency. Even without a physical museum, a “mini-museum corner” in the classroom, using student-made exhibits can bring lessons alive.

My reflections as a teacher

I have always believed that learning goes beyond the four walls of the classroom. Through differentiated instruction, active learning strategies, and multilingual approaches, I have encouraged students to engage, question, and explore concepts in ways that make learning meaningful and memorable. Imagining museums as extensions of the classroom fits naturally into this philosophy.

Collaborative approaches, such as working with the social science mentor to explore the historical and emotional background of lessons like Nelson Mandela: Long Walk to Freedom and From the Diary of Anne Frank, illustrate how cross-disciplinary thinking can deepen understanding. Even though I have not yet implemented full museum-based lessons, the ideas feel practical and promising. By treating museums – physical or virtual – as living classrooms, we can design activities that encourage observation, critical thinking, empathy, and creative expression. Learning becomes more human, connected, and joyful when students can see, touch, and feel the worlds they read about.

Conclusion: Taking English beyond four walls

A museum is not a separate world – it is an extended classroom that encourages exploration, reflection, and imagination. If teachers begin to see museums as learning partners, every visit or exhibit can become a lesson in empathy, expression, and awareness.

Museums remind us that learning is not about collecting information – it is about connecting with life itself. When students read, see, and feel, language takes on a deeper meaning, and English truly moves beyond the pages of the textbook.

Aparna D is a Post Graduate Teacher of English at NSN Memorial Senior Secondary School, Chennai. With 12 years of dedicated service, she believes in the philosophy “once a teacher, always a learner.” Through innovation, creativity, and values, she strives to make learning meaningful. From producing 100% results to training teachers and contributing to CBSE initiatives, she consistently goes the extra mile to empower learners holistically. She can be reached at aparnavishnuprasad@gmail.com.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Aparna D
Museums can become extensions of the English classroom, writes Aparna D. She shares examples of how teachers of classes 9 to 12 can connect literature with artefacts, history, and lived experiences.

item.title: What could a library teach about museums?
item.dc:creator: Kumar
item.content:encoded:
Radhika Chhaparia

The most desirable way to introduce children to a museum is to take them to one. However, when that isn’t possible, a trip to the library might also do the job. A well-curated collection of books, cards, documentaries, directories, and posters about the museums of India could help familiarize children with museums and arouse their curiosity to learn more about them. This article curates a few resources to that end. Unfortunately, my list here is limited to resources in the English language, with one exception. However, it is diverse in terms of the different facets of museums it introduces children to. Without further ado, allow me to walk you through the display.

A trip to the museum

A Day at the Museum is a comic book about five children, who visit a museum for the very first time and learn about its functioning and role in our society. Contrary to the commonly held opinion that museums are boring places, their trip turns out to be such a stimulating and inspiring experience that by the end of it, they each wish for careers in one.

Deepak, Gurtej, Hania, Monika, and Len are class 6 students of Sarvodaya Vidyalaya, New Delhi. Immediately after they finish writing their half-yearly exams, their school plans a class trip to the National Museum, New Delhi (NMND). Though some of them are looking forward to the trip, they can hardly anticipate the fascinating experience that awaits them. On their first day at the museum, they meet Ira, a curator and their guide for the day. While touring the ‘Harappa’ and ‘Sculpture’ galleries, they learn about how ‘found’ objects from ancient civilizations help understand the past. They also get first-hand experience of excavating buried objects from a sample pit specially set up for them, and later even create a record for their respective ‘found’ objects. At the end of the day, they return home with an independent project on their minds – to create a display of any object, similar to what they saw at the museum. On the second and final day of their trip, Dr. Patel, a conservator, introduces them to the conservation department at the museum. The children are surprised to learn that not only history but knowledge of chemistry too is essential for those working at the restoration side of museums. The trip leaves them inspired by the professionals they meet there, with a lingering passion towards preserving their own cultural heritage.

Visually immersive, informative, and craftfully scripted, the comic gives an overview of NMND. Readers get a glimpse of a museum which comes very close to being an ideal one: it’s inclusive (disability-friendly), offers quality educational and informative programs, is run by professionals, and is technologically advanced. The story is dialogue-rich and can be adapted to a play performance on the topic. But, the activity to match Indian gods with their vahanas (animal mounts) included at the end of the tour to the ‘Sculpture’ gallery seems completely out of place. Instead, an activity that tested the children’s ability to decipher code or form deductions from valuable objects rather than test their knowledge of Hindu gods would’ve been most suitable here. The information page about a few theme-based museums is a useful addition to the book, but it too is disappointing. Four out of five museums listed here belong to Delhi-NCR alone. Examples like Kerala Folklore Museum (Kochi), Calico Museum of Textiles (Gujarat), Assam Forest Museum (Guwahati), and Chennai Rail Museum (Chennai) would’ve provided a balanced representation of theme-based museums across India. A short documentary film on NMND available on its YouTube channel complements this comic well.

Sarayu’s Museum Adventure is an endearing picture storybook about a young girl’s impromptu and inspiring visit to the National Gallery of Modern Art (NGMA, New Delhi). When Sarayu has to draw a self-portrait for class, her mother, sensing her struggle, takes her to NGMA in search of some inspiration. In the art gallery there, Sarayu discovers artist Amrita Sher-Gil’s colour-filled portraits. They both marvel at the various emotions conveyed in her paintings. It seems a close look at Amrita’s paintings was just what Sarayu needed to complete her own portrait. Aimed at beginner readers, the book provides an easy window to the world of museums through the route of art galleries. It is best suited for a read-aloud with kindergarteners and for independent reading in case of primary schoolers. The book already includes an extension activity for drawing a self-portrait and a brief introduction to Amrita Sher-Gil, so teachers must take a call on introducing further resources. Aurko’s Museum Adventure with Abanindranath Tagore and Meera’s Museum Adventure with Raja Ravi Varma are two upcoming titles from the Museum Adventure series this book belongs to.

Investigating ancient art and monuments

Sculpted Stones – Mysteries of Mamallapuram is an interactive book which introduces children to rock-based sculptural art and monuments found at Mahabalipuram, dating back to the period between the 6th and 8th century CE. Declared a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1984, ‘The Group of Monuments’ is a collection of different types of temples and two open-air rock reliefs. Sure enough, the book sounds interesting, but why am I mentioning it here, in an article about museums? Firstly, The Group of Monuments is an archeological site with features(immovable material remains) from the past, and is in that respect similar to a collection of artefacts (movable material remains) found in museums. It holds clues about past human activity, art, culture, and society. The site is an ‘open-air museum of sorts’, a phrase which author Ashwin Prabhu himself passingly refers to in his book. Secondly, the approach towards studying the monuments demonstrated in this book is valuable for children who have just begun exploring historical narratives, facts, primary evidence and historical art; either in their history lessons at school or in other learning material. The thinking process we encounter throughout the book and particularly in the case of the open-air rock relief, popularly known as the ‘Great Penance’ or ‘Descent of the Ganges’ is exemplary in this regard.

Zoomed-in images of different parts of the relief are included alongside the author’s detailed observations, thoughts, questions and deductions. Young readers are made aware of other facts and resources the author refers to in order to present his narrative. Once children grasp Ashwin Prabhu’s investigative approach, they can easily participate by making their own observations and come up with questions before they read the text provided. The photographs in the book are clear enough to allow children’s participation. What else, you ask? We find not one but two interpretations about the Great Penance; a common phenomenon when studying historical art. Another useful takeaway from this book is the understanding that various considerations are involved in interpreting an antique: What is it made of? Where was it found? When and how was it made? What purpose did it serve then? Are any similar objects found elsewhere? Application of a similarly investigative approach, grounded in the understanding that objects originate in a socio-cultural and political context, would certainly enrich children’s trips to museums.

Another book with similar storytelling using sculptural art is While the women went hunting, what did the men do? What truly stands out about this book is the simultaneous use of historical evidence to dispel the commonly held myth about men as hunters. The writer, CN Subramaniam, connects sculptural art found at three different sites in which female figures are portrayed as hunters: Bhoramdev temple in Chhattisgarh, remains of palaces at Hampi in Karnataka, and Ahobilam temple in Andhra Pradesh. Here too, photographs are included for readers to observe details and follow the writer’s narrative. Again, we see the significance of research and knowledge of other resources like legends in forming deductions. But the observations and narrative are only a beginning point for the writer to raise deeper questions: Why did women go hunting? Was it a choice or a compulsion? Were they challenging social conventions even then or was it a norm? Who are the sculptures depicting – perhaps, the forest dwellers? What was the purpose behind this depiction? Answers to some of these questions, though hard to uncover, would only add nuance to stories about these sculptures. One way to introduce this book to children can be to exclude the text accompanying the 10 photographs from the monument at Hampi, and ask them to write a short story using their own observations before they read the book.

A story through objects

A Children’s History of India in 100 Objects is a curation of hundred objects, which together tell a unique story of the Indian sub-continent, starting from the prehistoric times and ending in the last decade of the 20th century. Each object is a standalone chapter in the book, and is brought to life with illustrations and a description which lays its entire context. Reminiscent of the BBC Radio series, ‘A History of the World in 100 Objects’ (2010), the display is in chronological order and tells a multidimensional story of humans from this region. Broadly, the book traces themes like ancient India, tribal life, religious traditions and structures, global trade, stories of invasions and liberation struggles, development of art and culture, independence from colonial rule, nation building, economic growth, and technological advancement. Similarly, the objects chosen are wide-ranging too: a terracotta monkey toy, a willow cricket bat, a carved wooden door, sculpted medallion on a stupa and a fragment of papyrus. Though the descriptions are detailed and full of jargon, Devika (archeologist-turned-writer), also provides simpler explanations. She explicitly conveys the implications of the developments taking place on the future of the Indian sub-continent, bearing in mind that children reading this book are certainly born after any of these objects and ideas took birth.

Unlike A Day at the Museum and Sarayu’s Museum Adventure mentioned earlier in the article, which provide a peek into the world of Indian museums, the experience of reading this book is akin to visiting a museum for a specially curated exhibition. It would be a daunting task to read this book cover to cover. Rather, savour it a few objects at a time. Later, explore some more objects from our collective past by visiting the museums from the list provided in the end pages.

Museum cards and directory

During the International Museum Expo 2023, organized by India’s Ministry of Culture in New Delhi, a few resources were developed: a Pictorial Biography on Museums, Directory of Indian Museums, Museum Cards, Pocket Map of Kartavya Path, and the Handbook on the Conservation of Tangible Cultural Resources. Each of these are available for download on the website of the National Council of Science Museums. Children can read them as they are or develop games and activities to gain familiarity with the museums of India.

Radhika Chhaparia has obtained an MA in Education from Azim Premji University, Bengaluru. She worked at Centre for Learning (CFL) school, Bengaluru, during the academic years 2020-23. She can be reached at radhika.chhaparia16_mae@apu.edu.in.
item.excerpt:encoded:
Radhika Chhaparia
Books and stories can help introduce children to India’s museums. Radhika Chhaparia presents a curated guide to books that bring the museum experience to children.

item.title: Carols, Chaos and Secret Santa: A Teacher’s Christmas Chronicles
item.dc:creator: shalini
item.content:encoded:
Priyanka Soman

December in school is a season of organized chaos disguised as festive cheer. As a teacher, I’ve had the joy—and occasional mischief—of celebrating Christmas with my students and colleagues, observing everything from glitter explosions to carol rehearsals that could double as comedy sketches.

The preparations begin weeks in advance. Classrooms transform into miniature winter wonderlands—or slightly lopsided Christmas corners. Tinsel migrates to unexpected places, baubles wobble dangerously on trees, and glitter somehow finds its way onto everyone’s uniform. Watching students decorate is like supervising a circus act: enthusiasm, chaos, and the occasional “Oh no, the star fell again!” moment.

One of the most delightful parts is the Carol Assembly. Weeks of rehearsals build to a grand performance where students sing with gusto, sometimes improvising lyrics that are equal parts hilarious and heartfelt. My favourite line this year: “Jingle all the way, with joy and cheer today!” There’s always that magical moment when a class forgets the words entirely and everyone hums along, proving enthusiasm beats perfect pitch any day.

Then comes the Secret Santa, a festival highlight. Permission from the school authorities ensures it’s inclusive and safe, but the guessing game begins immediately. Who gave the chocolate bar? Who gifted the quirky pencil? Teachers join in too, often turning it into a friendly competition for the funniest or most imaginative gift. Watching students’ wide-eyed speculation and colleagues’ playful rivalry is heartwarming—and endlessly amusing.

Food is another important part of the celebration. Festive treats—cookies, fruitcake, and the occasional homemade surprise—appear in classrooms, prompting delighted squeals and the inevitable “just one more slice” plea. Sharing these small treats reinforces the true spirit of Christmas: generosity, community, and a little indulgence.

What I love most about Christmas in school is the togetherness. It is in the laughter over slightly crooked decorations, the pride in a perfectly sung carol, and the warmth of Secret Santa exchanges. The festive spirit is colourful, occasionally chaotic, and always heartfelt.

Celebrating Christmas at school reminds me that the season is not just about decorations or presents—it’s about creating memories, sharing laughter, and enjoying those small moments that make school life magical. So, this December, join in the carols, embrace the classroom chaos, and celebrate with students and colleagues alike. After all, in school, Christmas isn’t just celebrated—it is lived, laughed over, and jingle all the way!

Priyanka Soman is an educator and writer with a keen interest in integrating the arts, sciences, and storytelling to create meaningful learning experiences. She can be reached at cspriyankaa@gmail.com.

Priyanka Soman
It is Christmas time! It is time for cheer and celebrations. What do Christmas celebrations in school mean for you?
